## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Differentiate between homofermentative and heterofermentative lactic acid bacteria with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Homofermentative LAB:** Convert glucose almost entirely (≥ 85%) to lactic acid via the **Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) pathway**. Key product: 2 lactate / 1 glucose. **Example:** *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus*, *Streptococcus thermophilus* (1 mark).
- **Heterofermentative LAB:** Use the **phosphoketolase (PK) pathway** to produce equimolar amounts of **lactic acid + CO₂ + ethanol (or acetate)**. **Example:** *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*, *Lactobacillus fermentum* (1 mark).
- **Distinguishing enzyme:** Homofermenters have **aldolase** (no PK); heterofermenters have **phosphoketolase** (no full EMP). Industrial significance: heterofermenters give flavor diversity in sauerkraut/kimchi (1 mark).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Why is the phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) step considered the rate-limiting and committed step of glycolysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Rate-limiting:** PFK-1 catalyses an **irreversible, far-from-equilibrium** reaction with the largest negative ΔG in glycolysis (1 mark).
- **Committed step:** Once fructose-1,6-bisphosphate is formed, the carbon is committed to the glycolytic pathway; no alternative fate exists (1 mark).
- **Allosteric regulation:** Activated by **AMP and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate**; inhibited by **ATP, citrate, and H⁺**. This makes PFK-1 the **central metabolic valve** matching glycolytic flux to cellular energy demand (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Never** quote ATP/glucose of "38" or "36" — KTU 2024 accepts only modern P/O ratios (2.5 NADH, 1.5 FADH₂), giving **30–32** (bacterial) or **~19.5** (eukaryotic shuttle-dependent).
> 2. **Always** show the net reaction in the form of a balanced chemical equation; do not just name enzymes.
> 3. In alcoholic fermentation, **CO₂ comes from pyruvate decarboxylation**, not from the TCA cycle.
> 4. In heterofermentative LAB, **CO₂ is released at the 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase step** (oxidative pentose phosphate), not at PDc.
> 5. In Part B (14 marks), KTU awards **7 marks per sub-part**. Always split your answer into two equal halves. **Do not write 4 pages for part (a) and 1 page for part (b).**
> 6. Use the **Pasteur equation** correctly: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \to 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$. Many students incorrectly write the coefficient of CO₂ as 1.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, PEFTT528 Module 2]**

**Q A (a).** Describe the Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) pathway in detail, highlighting the energy-investment and energy-payoff phases, the two ATP-generating steps, and the net ATP yield. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The EMP pathway (glycolysis) is a universal ten-step cytosolic catabolism converting one glucose to two pyruvates, yielding a net 2 ATP and 2 NADH. It was elucidated by **Gustav Embden, Otto Meyerhof, and Jakub Parnas** in the 1930s.

**2. Energy Investment Phase (Steps 1–5, 2 ATP consumed) (3 marks):**
- **Step 1 — Hexokinase (or glucokinase in liver):** Glucose + ATP → G6P + ADP. Commits glucose to intracellular metabolism.
- **Step 2 — Phosphoglucose isomerase:** G6P ⇌ F6P.
- **Step 3 — Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1):** F6P + ATP → FBP + ADP. **The committed, rate-limiting step**; allosterically regulated.
- **Step 4 — Aldolase:** FBP → DHAP + G3P (cleaves the 6-C sugar into two 3-C trioses).
- **Step 5 — Triose phosphate isomerase:** DHAP ⇌ G3P (this equilibrium allows both trioses to feed forward).

**3. Energy Payoff Phase (Steps 6–10, 4 ATP + 2 NADH produced per glucose) (3 marks):**
- **Step 6 — Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH):** G3P + NAD⁺ + Pᵢ → 1,3-BPG + NADH + H⁺. **First oxidative (NADH-forming) step.**
- **Step 7 — Phosphoglycerate kinase (PGK):** 1,3-BPG + ADP → 3-PG + ATP. **First ATP-generating step (substrate-level phosphorylation).** Doubled per glucose = 2 ATP.
- **Step 8 — Phosphoglycerate mutase:** 3-PG ⇌ 2-PG.
- **Step 9 — Enolase:** 2-PG → PEP + H₂O.
- **Step 10 — Pyruvate kinase (PK):** PEP + ADP → Pyruvate + ATP. **Second ATP-generating step.** Doubled = 2 ATP.

**Net yield per glucose: 2 ATP + 2 NADH + 2 Pyruvate + 2 H₂O.** (Sum 7 marks.)

---

**Q A (b).** With balanced chemical equations, explain **alcoholic fermentation** and **homofermentative lactic acid fermentation**. Compare their ATP yields and industrial product profiles. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Alcoholic Fermentation (3 marks):**
- **Microbe:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (also *Z. mobilis*).
- **Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)}\;\; CH_3COCOO^- + H^+ &\xrightarrow[\text{TPP, Mg}^{2+}]{\text{Pyruvate decarboxylase}} CH_3CHO + CO_2 \\
\text{(ii)}\;\; CH_3CHO + NADH + H^+ &\xrightarrow{\text{Alcohol dehydrogenase}} C_2H_5OH + NAD^+
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Net (Pasteur equation):** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \to 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$.
- **Products:** Ethanol (51.1% w/w theoretical), CO₂ (48.9% w/w theoretical).
- **Industrial applications:** Beer, wine, bread, sake, bioethanol, distilled spirits. The CO₂ is the leavening agent in bread.

**2. Homofermentative Lactic Acid Fermentation (3 marks):**
- **Microbes:** *Lactobacillus delbrueckii*, *L. bulgaricus*, *Streptococcus thermophilus*, *L. lactis*.
- **Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CH_3COCOO^- + NADH + H^+ \xrightarrow{\text{Lactate dehydrogenase}} CH_3CHOHCOO^- + NAD^+
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Net:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \to 2\,CH_3CHOHCOOH$ (theoretical mass yield = 100%).
- **Products:** Lactic acid (≥ 85% of carbon), giving pH drop to ~4.5.
- **Industrial applications:** Yogurt, cheese, sour cream, kefir, pickles, silage, sourdough (mixed cultures).

**3. Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Alcoholic | Homofermentative Lactic |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------------|
| Microbe | *S. cerevisiae* | *Lb. delbrueckii* |
| Key enzyme | Pyruvate decarboxylase | Lactate dehydrogenase |
| Gas evolved | CO₂ (2 mol/mol glucose) | None |
| Acid product | None (ethanol is neutral) | Lactic acid (2 mol/mol glucose) |
| Theoretical ATP | 2 ATP | 2 ATP |
| Theoretical mass yield | 0.51 g ethanol / g glucose | 1.00 g lactate / g glucose |
| Industrial food use | Beer, wine, bread | Yogurt, cheese, pickles |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, PEFTT528 Module 2]**

**Q B (a).** Draw and describe the **TCA (Krebs) cycle** with all eight enzymes, the three NADH-forming steps, the FADH₂-forming step, and the GTP-forming step. State the total ATP yield per acetyl-CoA using modern P/O ratios. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Overview (1 mark):**
The TCA cycle (citric acid cycle, Krebs cycle) is an 8-step, **strictly aerobic** pathway in the mitochondrial matrix (or bacterial cytoplasm). It oxidizes acetyl-CoA to 2 CO₂, harvesting **3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP**.

**2. The eight steps (5 marks):**

- **Step 1 — Citrate synthase:** OAA + Acetyl-CoA + H₂O → Citrate + CoA-SH. **Irreversible, exergonic.** Provides entry control.
- **Step 2 — Aconitase:** Citrate ⇌ Isocitrate (via cis-aconitate intermediate). Contains Fe-S cluster.
- **Step 3 — Isocitrate dehydrogenase (ICDH):** Isocitrate + NAD⁺ → α-Ketoglutarate + CO₂ + NADH. **First NADH + CO₂ release.**
- **Step 4 — α-Ketoglutarate dehydrogenase (α-KGDH):** α-KG + CoA + NAD⁺ → Succinyl-CoA + CO₂ + NADH. **Second NADH + CO₂ release.** TPP, lipoamide, FAD-dependent multi-enzyme complex.
- **Step 5 — Succinyl-CoA synthetase (succinate thiokinase):** Succinyl-CoA + GDP + Pᵢ → Succinate + GTP + CoA. **GTP/ATP-generating substrate-level phosphorylation.**
- **Step 6 — Succinate dehydrogenase (SDH):** Succinate + FAD → Fumarate + FADH₂. **FADH₂-producing step.** Membrane-bound; part of Complex II of the ETC.
- **Step 7 — Fumarase (fumarate hydratase):** Fumarate + H₂O ⇌ L-Malate.
- **Step 8 — Malate dehydrogenase (MDH):** Malate + NAD⁺ → OAA + NADH. **Third NADH-producing step.**

**3. Net equation per acetyl-CoA (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CH_3CO\text{-}SCoA + 3\,NAD^+ + FAD + GDP + P_i + 2\,H_2O \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,NADH + FADH_2 + GTP + CoA\text{-}SH
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. ATP Yield (modern P/O ratios) (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ATP_{TCA} &= 3 \times 2.5 + 1 \times 1.5 + 1 \times 1.0 \\
&= 7.5 + 1.5 + 1.0 = 10.0\;\text{ATP per acetyl-CoA}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Q B (b).** Explain **anaerobic respiration** in food-fermenting microbes. Compare **mixed-acid** and **2,3-butanediol** fermentations of the Enterobacteriaceae, giving at least two biochemical tests that differentiate them. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Anaerobic respiration regenerates NAD⁺ from NADH without oxygen by transferring electrons to organic (or inorganic) terminal acceptors. In food fermentations, pyruvate (or its derivatives) is the electron acceptor.

**2. Mixed-Acid Fermentation (2.5 marks):**
- **Genera:** *Escherichia*, *Salmonella*, *Shigella*.
- **Key enzyme:** **Pyruvate formate lyase (PFL)** — oxygen-sensitive, converts pyruvate to acetyl-CoA + formate.
- **Products (per 1 mol glucose):** Lactate (variable), acetate, formate, succinate, ethanol, **H₂** + **CO₂** (formate cleaved by **formate hydrogen lyase, FHL**).
- **Net pattern:** ~1 mol each of lactate, acetate, formate, ethanol + CO₂ + H₂.
- **pH drop:** Strong, due to high acid production.

**3. 2,3-Butanediol Fermentation (2.5 marks):**
- **Genera:** *Enterobacter*, *Klebsiella*, *Serratia*.
- **Key enzyme:** **α-Acetolactate synthase** and **acetolactate decarboxylase**; final step: **2,3-butanediol dehydrogenase**.
- **Products:** Acetoin, 2,3-butanediol, **less acid**, more neutral by-products.
- **Net pattern:** 2,3-BD as major product, smaller amounts of lactate, ethanol, CO₂.
- **Industrial relevance:** Basis for industrial 2,3-BD production (used as a solvent and jet fuel precursor).

**4. Differentiating biochemical tests (1 mark):**
- **Methyl Red (MR) test:** Mixed-acid producers (e.g., *E. coli*) → **MR positive** (acid, pH < 4.4, red color). 2,3-BD producers (e.g., *Enterobacter*) → **MR negative** (low acid).
- **Voges–Proskauer (VP) test:** 2,3-BD producers → **VP positive** (acetoin + diacetyl + guanidine → pink color). Mixed-acid producers → **VP negative**.
- **Gas from glucose (Durham tube):** Both produce gas, but 2,3-BD produces more CO₂ per mole acid.

**Summary equation (mixed-acid):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CHOHCOOH + CH_3COOH + HCOOH + C_2H_5OH + CO_2 + H_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary equation (2,3-BD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\,C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CHOHCHOHCH_3 + 2\,CO_2 + \text{smaller acids}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Sum 7 marks.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glycolysis (EMP)**
> - 10 enzymatic steps; 2 phases (investment 2 ATP → payoff 4 ATP).
> - **Net:** 2 ATP + 2 NADH + 2 Pyruvate per glucose.
> - **PFK-1** = rate-limiting, committed step.
> - **Hexokinase** (specificity, low $K_m$); **glucokinase** (high $K_m$, in liver/yeast).
> - **Pyruvate kinase** = second major regulatory step; activated by F1,6BP (feed-forward); inhibited by ATP and alanine.
> - **GAPDH** is the only oxidative (NADH-forming) step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TCA Cycle (Krebs / Citric Acid Cycle)**
> - **Aerobic only**; mitochondrial in eukaryotes, cytoplasmic in prokaryotes.
> - **8 steps, 3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP + 2 CO₂ per acetyl-CoA.**
> - **Citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase, α-KG dehydrogenase** are the three major regulatory enzymes.
> - **Succinate dehydrogenase** is the only membrane-bound enzyme (Complex II of ETC).
> - **Total ATP per acetyl-CoA = 10** (modern P/O); per glucose via TCA = 20; total aerobic (with link + glycolysis) = **30–32 ATP (prokaryote)** or **~19.5 ATP (eukaryote, shuttle-dependent)**.
> - **Amphibolic** nature: provides precursors for biosynthesis (α-KG → glutamate; OAA → aspartate; succinyl-CoA → heme).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anaerobic Respiration / Fermentation**
> - **Homofermentative LAB** → 2 lactate / glucose (100% mass); EMP pathway; **no gas**.
> - **Heterofermentative LAB** → 1 lactate + 1 ethanol + 1 CO₂ / glucose; **phosphoketolase** pathway; **gas + acid**.
> - **Alcoholic fermentation** → 2 ethanol + 2 CO₂ / glucose; *S. cerevisiae* / *Z. mobilis*; **PDc** (TPP) + **ADH**.
> - **Mixed-acid** (*E. coli*) → multiple acids; PFL; FHL; **MR+, VP−**.
> - **2,3-Butanediol** (*Enterobacter*, *Klebsiella*) → neutral; **MR−, VP+**.
> - **ATP yield = 2 ATP / glucose** for all fermentative routes.
> - **Pasteur effect:** anaerobic glycolytic flux is 4–7× higher than aerobic.
> - **Crabtree effect:** Some yeasts (e.g., *S. cerevisiae*) ferment even in presence of O₂ at high glucose — exploited in baker's yeast and brewing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Numerical Memorization for KTU**
> - Theoretical mass yield ethanol = **51.1% w/w**; lactate = **100% w/w**; CO₂ = **48.9% w/w**.
> - Aerobic efficiency ≈ **56%**; fermentative efficiency ≈ **3.5%**.
> - P/O ratios: **2.5 (NADH), 1.5 (FADH₂), 1.0 (GTP/ATP)**.
> - **PFK-1 ΔG ≈ −23 kJ/mol** (largest in glycolysis).
> - **Hexokinase $K_m$ glucose ≈ 0.1 mM**; **glucokinase $K_m$ glucose ≈ 10 mM**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food-Fermentation Connections to Remember**
> - **Yogurt:** *S. thermophilus* (homo, fast acid) + *Lb. bulgaricus* (homo, slow acid, flavor) — protocooperation.
> - **Sauerkraut/Kimchi:** *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* (hetero, initiates) → *Lb. plantarum* (homo, finishes) — succession.
> - **Sourdough:** *Lb. sanfranciscensis* (hetero) + *Candida humilis* (yeast) — symbiosis.
> - **Beer/Wine:** *S. cerevisiae* (alcoholic) — Crabtree-positive.
> - **Vinegar:** *Acetobacter* (strict aerobe, oxidizes ethanol via membrane-bound ADH + ALDH).
> - **Soy sauce:** *Aspergillus oryzae* (aerobic, hydrolytic) + LAB + yeast (fermentative).
> - **Swiss cheese:** *Propionibacterium freudenreichii* (propionic fermentation; CO₂ forms "eyes").
> - **Blue cheese:** *Penicillium roqueforti* (aerobic, lipolytic, methyl ketone formation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of Metabolites: Ethanol, Lactic Acid, Acetic Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT528 - FOOD FERMENTATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production of Metabolites: Ethanol, Lactic Acid, Acetic Acid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Microbial Metabolites — The Building Blocks of Industrial Fermentation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Metabolites* are the low-molecular-weight intermediates and products of microbial metabolism, classified broadly into **Primary Metabolites** (formed during the active growth/trophophase, e.g., ethanol, lactic acid, acetic acid, amino acids) and **Secondary Metabolites** (formed during the idiophase, e.g., antibiotics, pigments). The three target compounds of this module — **Ethanol, Lactic Acid, and Acetic Acid** — are quintessential primary metabolites produced in enormous bulk tonnages globally for food, fuel, and pharmaceutical industries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Microbe as a Tiny Chemical Factory"**
> Imagine a single yeast cell as a microscopic sugar-digestion factory. It consumes molasses (raw material) and, through its internal machinery (enzymes), converts it into ethanol (product) and CO₂ (by-product) — just like a factory takes in raw material and outputs a finished good, generating exhaust gas in the process. Lactic acid bacteria are like "yogurt-making specialists" that turn milk sugar into tangy acid, while acetic acid bacteria (vinegar makers) are "oxidation experts" that turn weak wine into sharp vinegar using oxygen. Each microbe is a specialist — its *identity* (genus/species) and *working conditions* (oxygen, pH, temperature) decide what final product you get.

### 1.2 Classification of the Three Target Metabolites

| Metabolite | Chemical Formula | Molecular Weight | Microbial Class | Type of Fermentation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ethanol** | C₂H₅OH | 46.07 g/mol | Yeast (Fungi) | Anaerobic |
| **Lactic Acid** | C₃H₆O₃ (CH₃CHOHCOOH) | 90.08 g/mol | Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB) | Anaerobic (mostly) |
| **Acetic Acid** | CH₃COOH (C₂H₄O₂) | 60.05 g/mol | Acetic Acid Bacteria (AAB) | Strictly Aerobic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these three are KTU Exam Favorites:**
> They represent the **three fundamental paradigms of industrial fermentation**:
> 1. **Ethanol** → substrate-level phosphorylation / yeast glycolysis (largest biofuel molecule)
> 2. **Lactic Acid** → homofermentative & heterofermentative LAB pathways (key for dairy industry & PLA bioplastics)
> 3. **Acetic Acid** → oxidative fermentation / incomplete oxidation by AAB (vinegar industry cornerstone)
> Mastery of these three gives a complete picture of *fermentative*, *oxidative*, and *anoxic* microbial production.

### 1.3 Industrial Significance Snapshot

- **Ethanol**: Global production > **100 billion litres/year** (2023 figures); major use is **biofuel (E10, E20 blends)** and **potable spirits**. Theoretical yield from glucose = **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose**.
- **Lactic Acid**: Global market projected **> 1.5 million tonnes/year**; ~50% used for **polylactic acid (PLA)** biodegradable polymer.
- **Acetic Acid**: Vinegar is the most consumed acidic condiment worldwide (> **20 million tonnes** glacial acetic acid equivalent annually).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ETHANOL — Production by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*

#### 2.1.1 The Microbial Workhorse

*Saccharomyces cerevisiae* is a **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** budding yeast that performs alcoholic fermentation. It thrives under:
- **Temperature:** 30 – 35 °C (optimum 30 °C)
- **pH:** 4.0 – 5.0 (acidic; suppresses contamination)
- **Oxygen:** Microaerobic / anaerobic (oxygen required only initially for sterol and unsaturated fatty acid synthesis)
- **Substrate:** Hexoses (glucose, fructose, sucrose, maltose) — *S. cerevisiae* cannot ferment pentoses directly

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does S. cerevisiae prefer low oxygen?** Oxygen activates the *Pasteur Effect* — it represses pyruvate decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase, redirecting carbon through respiration (CO₂ + H₂O) rather than fermentation. Industrialists therefore maintain strict anaerobiosis to maximize ethanol yield.

#### 2.1.2 Biochemistry — Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) Pathway

$$\text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{Glycolysis (10 steps)}} 2 \text{ Pyruvate} + 2 \text{ ATP} + 2 \text{ NADH}$$

$$\text{Pyruvate} \xrightarrow{\text{Pyruvate Decarboxylase (PDC)}} \text{Acetaldehyde} + \text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{Acetaldehyde} \xrightarrow{\text{Alcohol Dehydrogenase (ADH)}} \text{Ethanol} + \text{NAD}^+$$

**Net Reaction (Anoxic):**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 + \text{Energy (Heat)}$$

#### 2.1.3 Industrial Process Steps

1. **Feedstock Preparation** — Molasses diluted to 15–20% w/v sugar; pH adjusted to 4.5 with H₂SO₄; nutrient supplementation (urea, ammonium salts, Mg²⁺).
2. **Sterilization** — Pasteurization at 80–90 °C for 15–30 min to eliminate wild contaminants.
3. **Inoculation** — 5–10% v/v active yeast slurry (rehydrated dry yeast or propagated seed).
4. **Fermentation Modes:**
   - **Batch** (most common; 48–72 h; ~8–12% v/v ethanol)
   - **Fed-Batch** (very high gravity; sugar fed to maintain < 100 g/L to avoid osmotic stress)
   - **Continuous (CSTR)** (rare; concerns of contamination and strain degeneration)
5. **Cell Separation** — Centrifugation or sedimentation; yeast can be recycled.
6. **Distillation** — Beer (8–12% alcohol) → Rectified Spirit (95.6% v/v) via fractional distillation.
7. **Dehydration** — Molecular sieves (3 Å zeolite) to break the 95.6% azeotrope → **Absolute Alcohol (99.9% v/v)**.

#### 2.1.4 End-Product Inhibition (Product Toxicity)

Ethanol is **lipophilic** and disrupts yeast plasma membrane integrity. Critical threshold:

$$\text{Inhibition threshold: } C_{EtOH}^{crit} \approx 110 - 120 \text{ g/L} \;(\approx 14\% \text{ v/v})$$

Strategies to overcome: cell immobilization, vacuum fermentation, membrane bioreactors, strain engineering for osmotolerance.

---

### 2.2 LACTIC ACID — Production by Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB)

#### 2.2.1 The Microorganisms

- **Homofermentative:** *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus*, *L. plantarum*, *L. casei*, *L. acidophilus* — produce > **90% lactic acid** from glucose.
- **Heterofermentative:** *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*, *Lactobacillus brevis* — produce lactic acid + CO₂ + ethanol/acetic acid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question — Distinguish Homo vs Hetero:**
> - **Homofermentative** LAB use the **Embden-Meyerhof (EMP) pathway** exclusively → yield 2 mol lactic acid per mol glucose.
> - **Heterofermentative** LAB use the **6-phosphogluconate / phosphoketolase pathway** → yield only 1 mol lactic acid + 1 mol CO₂ + 1 mol ethanol/acetic acid per mol glucose. **Hence heterofermenters are biotechnologically less efficient for industrial LA production.**

#### 2.2.2 Biochemistry

**Homofermentative Stoichiometry:**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} \quad \Delta G^\circ = -198 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{Yield (theoretical): } \frac{2 \times 90.08}{180.16} = 1.0 \text{ g LA / g glucose} \;(100\% \text{ theoretical})$$

**Heterofermentative Stoichiometry (one canonical form):**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2$$

#### 2.2.3 Two Stereoisomers — L(+) and D(−) — Critical for KTU

Lactic acid exists as two enantiomers, and LAB produce them in varying ratios:
- **L(+) Lactic Acid** — produced by *L. casei*, *L. plantarum*, *L. delbrueckii* (most LAB). Preferred for **human metabolism** (L-lactate dehydrogenase in liver). Essential for **food-grade PLA**.
- **D(−) Lactic Acid** — produced by *L. bulgaricus*, *L. lactis*. Can cause acidosis if ingested in humans. PLA made from D-lactate is undesirable for medical implants.

> [!NOTE]
> **For PLA Bioplastics:** Optically pure L(+) lactic acid is preferred because PLA synthesis via ring-opening polymerization of lactide requires high enantiomeric purity (> 99%). Industrial strains are engineered to over-produce pure L-lactic acid.

#### 2.2.4 Process Conditions

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Temperature | 30 – 45 °C (species-dependent) |
| pH | 5.5 – 6.5 (controlled with CaCO₃ or NaOH) |
| O₂ | Anaerobic to microaerobic |
| Substrate | Glucose, sucrose, whey lactose, starch hydrolysate |
| Fermentation time | 24 – 96 h |
| pH control | **CaCO₃ neutralization → Ca-lactate precipitation** (most common) |

#### 2.2.5 Downstream Recovery

Lactic acid recovery is the most expensive step (~50% of total production cost). Methods:
1. **Precipitation** as **calcium lactate** (CaCO₃ added during fermentation) → acidified with H₂SO₄ → crude LA → purification.
2. **Reactive extraction** (e.g., Alamine 336 in oleyl alcohol).
3. **Ion exchange** chromatography.
4. **Electrodialysis** (modern, green, expensive membranes).
5. **Membrane separation / Nanofiltration.**

---

### 2.3 ACETIC ACID — Vinegar Production by Acetic Acid Bacteria (AAB)

#### 2.3.1 The Microorganisms

- ***Acetobacter aceti*** — most common industrial vinegar bacterium.
- ***Gluconobacter oxydans*** — preferred for surface fermentations.
- All AAB are **gram-negative, obligate aerobes, rod-shaped**, belonging to family *Acetobacteraceae*.
- Unique feature: they **do not oxidize organic substrates completely** to CO₂; they perform **incomplete oxidation**, leaving organic acids as end products. This is called **"oxidative fermentation."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:**
> **Oxidative Fermentation** = A fermentation in which the substrate is incompletely oxidized by a strict aerobe using molecular O₂ as the terminal electron acceptor, and the partially oxidized organic acid is accumulated as the end product (e.g., ethanol → acetic acid, sorbitol → sorbose, glycerol → dihydroxyacetone). This is industrially exploited in **vinegar production**.

#### 2.3.2 Biochemistry — Two-Step Aerobic Oxidation

$$\text{Step 1: } \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Alcohol Dehydrogenase (ADH)}} \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

$$\text{Step 2: } \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Aldehyde Dehydrogenase (ALDH)}} \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$

**Net Reaction:**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta G^\circ = -455 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{Yield (theoretical): } \frac{60.05}{46.07} = 1.304 \text{ g acetic acid / g ethanol}$$

#### 2.3.3 Industrial Vinegar Processes

1. **Orleans Process (Slow Surface Process)** — historically the oldest. Wine/cider held in barrels partially filled; *Acetobacter* forms a *vinegar mother* (cellulosic pellicle) on the surface. Slow: weeks to months. Gives premium product (Balsamic tradition).
2. **Frings Acetator (Submerged Process)** — modern, rapid. Forced aeration via spargers, mechanical agitation, **turbine impeller + Frings aerator** creates ultra-fine bubble dispersion. Fermentation time: **24–48 h**. Acidity: 12–15% w/v. This is the global industry standard.
3. **Trickling Generator (Vinegar Generator)** — fixed-film bioreactor where ethanol solution trickles over *beechwood shavings* or *corn-cob carriers* on which *Acetobacter* biofilm is attached. Counter-current air flow. Medium speed.
4. **Submerged with Cell Recycle (Cavitator)** — high-cell-density, continuous.
5. **Semi-continuous / Continuous** with feedback of fermenting broth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Frings Acetator dominates global production:**
> 1. Oxygen transfer rate is the **rate-limiting step** in acetic fermentation (oxygen solubility in water is only ~8 mg/L). The Frings' unique rotor-stator self-aspirating aerator achieves **kLa > 500 h⁻¹**, far higher than standard Rushton turbines.
> 2. Temperature must be **controlled at 28–30 °C** (higher temperatures kill AAB).
> 3. Final acidity must be kept **< 15% w/v** because at > 16% AAB cells undergo plasmolysis and lose viability.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS BLOCK**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Theoretical Ethanol Yield** | $Y_{EtOH/S} = \frac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16}$ | **0.511 g/g glucose** (or 0.646 g/g sucrose) |
| 2 | **Theoretical Lactic Acid Yield (Homo)** | $Y_{LA/S} = \frac{2 \times 90.08}{180.16}$ | **1.000 g/g glucose** (100%) |
| 3 | **Theoretical Lactic Acid Yield (Hetero)** | $Y_{LA/S} = \frac{1 \times 90.08}{180.16}$ | **0.500 g/g glucose** (50%) |
| 4 | **Theoretical Acetic Acid Yield** | $Y_{AA/EtOH} = \frac{60.05}{46.07}$ | **1.303 g acetic acid / g ethanol** |
| 5 | **Actual vs Theoretical Yield** | $\eta = \frac{Y_{actual}}{Y_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Ethanol: 90–95% typical |
| 6 | **Volumetric Productivity** | $Q_p = \frac{C_{product}}{t_{fermentation}}$ | Ethanol: 1.5–3 g/L·h |
| 7 | **Product Concentration** | $C_p = C_{s,0} \times Y_{P/S}$ | g/L |
| 8 | **Overall Mass Balance** | $C_{s,0} = C_{s,final} + C_p / Y_{P/S}$ | g substrate / L |
| 9 | **Cell Mass Yield (ethanol)** | $Y_{X/S} \approx 0.1$ g/g (low due to anaerobic) | g/g |
| 10 | **Growth Rate (Monod)** | $\mu = \mu_{max} \frac{S}{K_s + S}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 11 | **Substrate Saturation Constant (Yeast)** | $K_s$ for glucose | ~0.5 g/L |
| 12 | **Doubling Time** | $t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu_{max}}$ | Yeast: 1.5–2 h |
| 13 | **Oxygen Transfer Rate** | $OTR = k_La(C^* - C_L)$ | mg O₂/L·h |
| 14 | **Distillation Mass Balance** | $V_{spirit} = V_{beer} \times \left(\frac{C_{EtOH, beer}}{C_{EtOH, spirit}}\right)$ | Litres |
| 15 | **Azeotrope Composition** | 95.6% v/v ethanol / 4.4% water at 78.2 °C | % v/v |
| 16 | **ATP from EMP (ethanol)** | Net = 2 ATP per glucose | mol/mol |
| 17 | **Sterilization Decimal Reduction** | $D = \frac{t}{\log(N_0/N)}$ | minutes |
| 18 | **Heat Sterilization Criterion** | $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{(T-T_{ref})/z}$ | minutes |
| 19 | **Plackett-Burman for medium opt.** | Screening design | 8–12 runs |
| 20 | **Griffith's Yield Stress (Broth)** | $\tau_y$ for mycelial/non-Newtonian | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Conventions Used in KTU Papers:**
> - Concentrations: **g/L** (broth) or **% w/v** or **% v/v** (for ethanol) — always specify.
> - Yields: dimensionless (g/g) or g product per g substrate.
> - Productivity: **g L⁻¹ h⁻¹** or **kg m⁻³ h⁻¹** (multiply by 1.0 to convert).
> - Time: hours for fermentation; minutes for sterilization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose

**Problem Context (typical KTU numerical):** A fermentation broth contains 200 g/L of glucose initially. Calculate the maximum possible ethanol concentration (g/L) and % v/v assuming complete conversion.

#### Step 1 — Write the balanced stoichiometric equation

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

#### Step 2 — Determine the molar mass ratio

The molar mass of glucose = **180.16 g/mol**, and ethanol = **46.07 g/mol**.

Mass of 2 moles of ethanol = $2 \times 46.07 = 92.14 \text{ g}$

#### Step 3 — Compute the stoichiometric mass yield (Y_P/S)

The yield coefficient is the mass of product formed per mass of substrate consumed:

$$
Y_{P/S} = \frac{\text{Mass of product}}{\text{Mass of substrate consumed}} = \frac{92.14 \text{ g ethanol}}{180.16 \text{ g glucose}} = 0.511 \frac{\text{g}}{\text{g}}
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the mass balance to the broth

For every 180.16 g of glucose consumed, 92.14 g of ethanol is produced. Scale up to 200 g/L initial glucose:

$$
C_{EtOH} = C_{glucose, 0} \times Y_{P/S} = 200 \text{ g/L} \times 0.511 = 102.2 \text{ g/L}
$$

#### Step 5 — Convert g/L to % v/v (KTU frequently asks this)

The density of pure ethanol at 20 °C = **0.789 g/mL**. Volume of 102.2 g of ethanol:

$$
V_{EtOH} = \frac{102.2 \text{ g}}{0.789 \text{ g/mL}} = 129.5 \text{ mL} = 0.1295 \text{ L}
$$

Assuming the broth volume remains approximately 1 L (1 litre), % v/v = 12.95% v/v.

$$
\%\,v/v \approx 12.95\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight — *In reality* broth density is not exactly 1, and CO₂ evolution (44 g/L) reduces the broth volume. KTU's 14-mark numerical problems often ask students to include a correction factor, e.g., assume broth density = 1.005 g/mL to be safe.**

#### Step 6 — Actual industrial yield with realistic conversion efficiency

If the fermentation achieves 92% of theoretical (typical for industrial batch):

$$
C_{EtOH, actual} = 0.92 \times 102.2 = 94.0 \text{ g/L} \approx 11.92\%\ v/v
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lactic Acid Yield Comparison: Homo vs Hetero

**KTU-style question:** Compare the productivity and yield of lactic acid when 1 kg of glucose is fermented by (a) *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* (homofermentative) and (b) *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* (heterofermentative).

**Case (a) — Homofermentative EMP Pathway**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH}
$$

$$
Y_{LA/S} = \frac{2 \times 90.08}{180.16} = 1.000 \text{ g/g}
$$

From 1 kg glucose → 1.000 kg lactic acid (theoretical).

**Case (b) — Heterofermentative Phosphoketolase Pathway**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2
$$

$$
Y_{LA/S} = \frac{1 \times 90.08}{180.16} = 0.500 \text{ g/g}
$$

From 1 kg glucose → 0.500 kg lactic acid (theoretical).

**Conclusion (must include in KTU answer):** The heterofermentative pathway gives **half the yield** of the homofermentative pathway. Hence **homofermentative strains are commercially preferred for industrial LA production**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Acetic Acid from Ethanol Stoichiometry

**KTU-style numerical:** 500 L of 10% v/v ethanol broth is fermented to acetic acid using *Acetobacter aceti* in a Frings Acetator. Calculate (a) initial mass of ethanol, (b) theoretical mass of acetic acid produced, (c) % conversion if final acetic acid concentration is 11.5% w/v.

#### Step 1 — Convert initial 10% v/v ethanol to mass

Volume of pure ethanol in 500 L = 10% × 500 L = 50 L = 50,000 mL.

Mass of ethanol = 50,000 mL × 0.789 g/mL = **39,450 g = 39.45 kg**

#### Step 2 — Theoretical acetic acid mass

$$
\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

Stoichiometric ratio: 1 mol ethanol (46.07 g) → 1 mol acetic acid (60.05 g).

$$
Y_{AA/EtOH} = \frac{60.05}{46.07} = 1.303 \text{ g/g}
$$

$$
m_{AA, theoretical} = 39.45 \text{ kg} \times 1.303 = 51.40 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 3 — Final actual concentration in 500 L

If final broth = 11.5% w/v = 11.5 g per 100 mL = 115 g/L.

$$
m_{AA, actual} = 115 \text{ g/L} \times 500 \text{ L} = 57.50 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 4 — Conversion / Efficiency calculation

$$
\eta = \frac{m_{AA, actual}}{m_{AA, theoretical}} \times 100 = \frac{57.50}{51.40} \times 100 = 111.86\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🚩 This is a trick KTU question — The calculated value >100% is physically impossible!** The reason: the *initial ethanol* itself contributes to the broth's mass and volume. A more rigorous approach: track only the *consumed ethanol*. If final ethanol = 1% v/v, then 9% v/v was consumed. Revised mass of ethanol consumed = 9/10 × 39.45 = 35.51 kg → theoretical AA = 35.51 × 1.303 = 46.27 kg. % conversion = 57.50/46.27 × 100 = **124% (still impossible) — indicates a problem with the question or a data error in the original paper.** Always check your answer physically — yield cannot exceed theoretical maximum.

#### Step 5 — Industrial Realistic Conversion (Correct Approach)

A correctly framed KTU question gives the **initial and final ethanol** values:
- Initial: 10% v/v → 500 L → 39.45 kg EtOH
- Final: 1% v/v → 5 L pure EtOH in 500 L → 3.945 kg
- EtOH consumed = 39.45 − 3.945 = 35.51 kg
- Theoretical AA = 35.51 × 1.303 = 46.27 kg
- Actual AA (assuming broth density ~1.0): 57.50 kg **is the intended answer** if the question framed "produced acetic acid" — the % yield would be 57.50/(35.51 × 1.303) × 100 = **124.3%** (i.e., the question itself has an inconsistency).

The cleaner method: **% Conversion of Ethanol** = (39.45 − 3.945)/39.45 × 100 = **90%** ethanol consumed.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison of the Three Processes

| Parameter | **Ethanol** | **Lactic Acid** | **Acetic Acid** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microbe** | *S. cerevisiae* | *Lactobacillus* spp. (homo) | *Acetobacter* / *Gluconobacter* |
| **Domain** | Eukaryote (Fungi) | Prokaryote (Bacteria) | Prokaryote (Bacteria) |
| **O₂ Requirement** | Microaerobic | Anaerobic | **Strictly aerobic** |
| **Pathway** | EMP + PDC + ADH | EMP + LDH | ADH + ALDH (oxidative) |
| **Theoretical Yield (g/g)** | 0.511 (from glucose) | 1.000 (from glucose) | 1.303 (from ethanol) |
| **Practical Yield** | 90–95% of theoretical | 80–90% | 85–95% |
| **End-Product Inhibition** | Strong (> 110 g/L) | Moderate (low pH) | Strong (> 15% w/v) |
| **Typical Final Titer** | 8–12% v/v | 50–150 g/L | 10–15% w/v |
| **Fermentation Time** | 48–72 h | 24–96 h | 24–48 h (Frings) |
| **Optimum Temperature** | 30 °C | 37 °C (varies) | 28–30 °C |
| **Sterilization Needed?** | Yes (pasteurization) | Yes (HTST) | No (high acid protects) |
| **Reactor Type** | Stirred tank batch | Stirred tank fed-batch | Submerged aerated (Frings) |
| **Major Downstream Step** | Distillation + Molecular Sieve | Precipitation + Crystallization | Filtration + Aging |
| **Industrial Scale** | Mega (10⁷ L) | Large (10⁵ L) | Large (10⁵ L) |
| **Global Market Status** | Largest bio-product (>100 BL/y) | Fast growing (PLA) | Stable (vinegar) |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Productivity Calculation (Volumetric)

**KTU-style problem:** A 100,000 L bioreactor produces 12,000 kg of ethanol in 60 hours. Calculate the volumetric productivity.

$$
Q_p = \frac{\text{Mass of product}}{\text{Working volume} \times \text{Time}} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ kg}}{100{,}000 \text{ L} \times 60 \text{ h}}
$$

$$
Q_p = \frac{12{,}000}{6{,}000{,}000} \text{ kg/(L·h)} = 0.002 \text{ kg/(L·h)} = 2.0 \text{ g/(L·h)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical industrial benchmarks:** Batch ethanol Q_p = 1.5–3.0 g/L·h. Continuous stirred-tank ethanol Q_p = 5–10 g/L·h. Vacuum ethanol Q_p (with in-situ product removal) = 20–40 g/L·h.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 5 — Substrate Balance for an Industrial Batch

**Given:** 10,000 L of 22 °Brix (i.e., 22 g sugar / 100 g solution) molasses (density = 1.09 g/mL) is fermented by *S. cerevisiae*. Residual sugar at the end of fermentation = 1.5 g/100 g. Compute (a) initial and final total sugars in kg, (b) sugars consumed, (c) maximum ethanol produced assuming 90% of theoretical yield.

#### Step 1 — Initial mass of molasses

$$
m_{molasses,0} = 10{,}000 \text{ L} \times 1.09 \text{ kg/L} = 10{,}900 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 2 — Initial sugar mass

$$
m_{s,0} = 10{,}900 \times \frac{22}{100} = 2{,}398 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 3 — Final sugar mass

$$
m_{s,f} = 10{,}900 \times \frac{1.5}{100} = 163.5 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 4 — Sugar consumed

$$
\Delta m_s = 2{,}398 - 163.5 = 2{,}234.5 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 5 — Theoretical ethanol

$$
m_{EtOH, theor} = 2{,}234.5 \times 0.511 = 1{,}141.8 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 6 — Actual ethanol at 90% of theoretical

$$
m_{EtOH, actual} = 0.90 \times 1{,}141.8 = 1{,}027.6 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical yield (1,027.6 kg from 10,900 kg molasses) ≈ 9.4% w/w ethanol in broth.** This matches industrial batch reality for 22°Brix molasses.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish clearly between primary and secondary microbial metabolites. Give two industrial examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Primary Metabolites:** These are low-molecular-weight compounds produced during the **trophophase** (active growth phase) of a microorganism. They are essential for cellular growth, energy metabolism, and structural biosynthesis. Their production is directly coupled to growth (growth-associated kinetics). Examples: **Ethanol, lactic acid, acetic acid, amino acids (glutamic acid), citric acid, nucleotides**.

**Secondary Metabolites:** These are compounds produced during the **idiophase** (stationary phase), after the exponential growth has slowed. They are not essential for growth and their production is **non-growth-associated**. They often have complex chemical structures and include antibiotics, pigments, toxins, and hormones. Examples: **Penicillin, Streptomycin, Erythromycin**.

**Key Differences Table (for full marks):**

| Aspect | Primary | Secondary |
|---|---|---|
| Phase of Production | Trophophase | Idiophase |
| Coupled to Growth? | Yes (growth-associated) | No (non-growth-associated) |
| Function | Essential for growth | Ecological/defensive |
| Yield | High (g/L) | Low (mg/L) |
| Examples | Ethanol, LA, AA | Penicillin, Tetracycline |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why are homofermentative lactic acid bacteria preferred over heterofermentative strains for industrial lactic acid production?*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher Yield:** Homofermentative LAB use the EMP pathway exclusively, giving **theoretical yield of 1.0 g lactic acid per g glucose** (2 mol LA per mol glucose). Heterofermentative LAB use the phosphoketolase pathway giving only **0.5 g LA per g glucose** (1 mol LA per mol glucose + 1 mol CO₂ + 1 mol ethanol). The hetero route is therefore **50% less efficient**.

2. **Single Product:** Homofermenters produce only lactic acid as the fermentation end product, simplifying downstream recovery. Heterofermenters give mixed products (lactic acid + ethanol + CO₂) requiring expensive separation.

3. **Optical Purity:** Many industrial homofermenters (e.g., *L. casei*, *L. plantarum*) produce predominantly **L(+) lactic acid**, which is the preferred isomer for human food applications and PLA synthesis.

4. **Lower By-Product Formation:** No CO₂ evolution in homofermentation means less foaming in fermenters.

Thus, **homofermentative strains are the universal choice for industrial LA production**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 2 KTU Pattern**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

*Discuss in detail the production of industrial ethanol by fermentation. Your answer must include:*
*(a) [7 Marks] The microbial strain, its metabolic pathway for ethanol formation (with balanced equation and key enzymes), and the biochemical energy balance.*
*(b) [7 Marks] The complete industrial process flow — from raw material selection through fermentation, downstream processing, and purification to absolute alcohol.*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Microbial Strain and Metabolism [7 Marks]**

The workhorse organism for industrial ethanol production is ***Saccharomyces cerevisiae***, a budding yeast, classified under Kingdom Fungi, Ascomycota. It is **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** by the US FDA, has a long history of safe use in brewing and baking, and tolerates high ethanol and low pH — all desirable for industrial robustness.

**Process Parameters:**
- Temperature: 30 – 35 °C (optimum 30 °C)
- pH: 4.0 – 5.0 (natural self-acidifying broth)
- Oxygen: Microaerobic — small amounts of O₂ are needed at the start for the synthesis of sterols and unsaturated fatty acids for membrane biogenesis, but vigorous aeration **inhibits ethanol formation** (Pasteur Effect).
- Substrate: Hexose sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose, maltose) — *S. cerevisiae* **cannot ferment pentoses** (xylose, arabinose) directly, which is a key limitation when using lignocellulosic biomass.

**Metabolic Pathway — EMP + Alcoholic Fermentation:**

The complete pathway proceeds in 12 enzymatic steps from glucose to ethanol:

$$ \text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{Hexokinase}} \text{Glucose-6-P} \xrightarrow{\text{PGI}} \text{Fructose-6-P} \xrightarrow{\text{PFK}} \text{Fructose-1,6-BP} $$

$$ \text{Fructose-1,6-BP} \xrightarrow{\text{ALD}} 2 \text{ Glyceraldehyde-3-P} \xrightarrow{\text{GAPDH}} 2 \text{ 1,3-BPG} \xrightarrow{\text{PGK}} 2 \text{ 3-PG} $$

$$ 2 \text{ 3-PG} \xrightarrow{\text{PGM}} 2 \text{ 2-PG} \xrightarrow{\text{ENO}} 2 \text{ PEP} \xrightarrow{\text{PK}} 2 \text{ Pyruvate} $$

The 2 pyruvate molecules then undergo alcoholic fermentation:

$$ 2 \text{ Pyruvate} \xrightarrow{\text{Pyruvate Decarboxylase (PDC)}} 2 \text{ Acetaldehyde} + 2 \text{ CO}_2 $$

$$ 2 \text{ Acetaldehyde} \xrightarrow{\text{Alcohol Dehydrogenase (ADH)}} 2 \text{ Ethanol} $$

**Net Reaction:**

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{ATP} \;(\text{net}) $$

**Energy Balance:** Net gain of **2 ATP per glucose** (4 produced in lower glycolysis, 2 consumed in upper glycolysis). The actual **enthalpy of combustion** of the 2 moles of ethanol is ~2634 kJ; about 217 kJ is conserved in ATP and the rest is released as heat. The low ATP yield is why *S. cerevisiae* must ferment **enormous quantities of sugar** to sustain growth — this is also why ethanol production is cheap in terms of substrate cost.

**Theoretical Yield:**

$$ Y_{P/S} = \frac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = 0.511 \text{ g ethanol / g glucose} $$

---

**(b) Industrial Process Flow [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Raw Material Selection:** The most common feedstocks globally are:
- **Sugarcane molasses** (~50% fermentable sugar) — Brazil, India
- **Sugar beet molasses** — Europe
- **Corn starch hydrolysate** — USA (corn-milling wet process)
- **Whey lactose** — Dairy by-product utilization
- **Cassava, sweet sorghum, Jerusalem artichoke** — emerging feedstocks

**Step 2 — Feedstock Preparation:** Molasses is diluted to 15–20% w/v sugar, pH adjusted to 4.5 using H₂SO₄, supplemented with ammonium salts or urea (nitrogen source), MgSO₄, and trace elements.

**Step 3 — Pasteurization:** The diluted mash is heated to 80–90 °C for 15–30 min to eliminate wild yeast, bacteria, and mould spores. This step is critical because contamination diverts carbon away from ethanol.

**Step 4 — Inoculation:** 5–10% v/v of an active yeast culture (either fresh propagated or rehydrated dry yeast) is added. Modern plants use **5–10 kg of dry yeast per 100 m³ of broth**.

**Step 5 — Fermentation:** Conducted in large stainless-steel stirred-tank reactors (100–1000 m³). The fermentation is **exothermic** — temperature is controlled at 30 °C using external jackets. **CO₂ evolution is captured** (often liquefied and sold) as a valuable by-product. Fermentation time: 48–72 h. Final ethanol titer: 8–12% v/v (60–95 g/L).

**Step 6 — Cell Separation:** The fermented "beer" is centrifuged (or the yeast is allowed to sediment and recycled) to remove yeast cells. Spent yeast is sold as animal feed supplement.

**Step 7 — Distillation:** The clarified beer is fed into a multi-column distillation train:
- **Beer column** → removes dissolved solids and most water
- **Rectifying column** → concentrates ethanol to **95.6% v/v** (the ethanol-water azeotrope)
- **Extractive or azeotropic column** (optional) → for dehydrated alcohol

**Step 8 — Dehydration:** The 95.6% azeotrope cannot be broken by distillation alone. **Molecular sieves (3 Å zeolite beads)** are used to adsorb the residual water selectively, producing **absolute alcohol (99.5–99.9% v/v)**. This is the modern preferred method (energy-efficient vs older azeotropic distillation with benzene or cyclohexane).

**Final Products:** Biofuel (anhydrous ethanol blended with petrol), potable spirits, pharmaceutical-grade alcohol, industrial solvents, and cosmetics-grade ethanol.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Question Paper]**

*Describe the industrial production of vinegar (acetic acid) by the Frings submerged fermentation process. Your answer must include:*
*(a) [7 Marks] The microbial strain, its morphological and physiological characteristics, and the two-step biochemistry of acetic acid formation with balanced equations and key enzymes.*
*(b) [7 Marks] The detailed design and operation of the Frings Acetator — including oxygen transfer considerations, process parameters, and the critical role of Frings aerator technology.*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Microbial Strain and Biochemistry [7 Marks]**

Vinegar (acetic acid) is produced by **Acetic Acid Bacteria (AAB)** — a group of obligately aerobic, gram-negative, rod-shaped bacteria belonging to the family *Acetobacteraceae*. The two principal industrial genera are:

- ***Acetobacter aceti*** — used in submerged vinegar processes (Frings Acetator); strongly oxidizes ethanol; produces cellulose pellicle.
- ***Gluconobacter oxydans*** — preferred for surface (Orleans) process; oxidizes a wide range of sugars and alcohols incompletely.

**Morphological & Physiological Characteristics:**
- Shape: short rods (0.6–0.8 µm × 1.0–4.0 µm), occurring singly, in pairs, or short chains.
- Gram reaction: Gram-negative.
- Motility: peritrichous flagella (in *Acetobacter*).
- Metabolism: Strictly **aerobic**; **catalase-positive**; **oxidase-negative**.
- Habitat: Sugary, alcoholic, and acidic environments (fruits, flowers, spoiled wine, beer, cider).
- Unique trait: AAB possess **membrane-bound dehydrogenases** that perform **incomplete oxidation** of substrates, leaving organic acids (like acetic acid) as end products — this is called **oxidative fermentation**.

**Two-Step Biochemistry of Acetic Acid Formation:**

**Step 1 — Oxidation of ethanol to acetaldehyde:**

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{NAD(P)}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{Membrane-bound Alcohol Dehydrogenase (ADH)}} \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{NAD(P)H} + \text{H}^+ $$

**Step 2 — Oxidation of acetaldehyde to acetic acid:**

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{NADP}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Aldehyde Dehydrogenase (ALDH)}} \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{NADPH} $$

The reduced coenzymes (NADH, NADPH) are reoxidized via the **electron transport chain** with molecular O₂ as the final electron acceptor. The bacteria are poisoned by their own product; this is the basis of the **"vinegar injury"** at high acidities.

**Net Reaction:**

$$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Acetobacter}} \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta G^\circ = -455 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

**Theoretical Yield:**

$$ Y_{P/S} = \frac{60.05}{46.07} = 1.304 \text{ g acetic acid / g ethanol} $$

---

**(b) Frings Acetator — Design and Operation [7 Marks]**

The **Frings Acetator** is the global industry-standard bioreactor for vinegar production, developed by Heinrich Frings in 1950s. It is essentially a **submerged, aerated, agitated fermenter** with a unique self-aspirating aerator.

**Design Components:**

1. **Vertical Cylindrical Tank** (typically 20–100 m³ working volume; largest units up to 200 m³) made of stainless steel (grade 304) or fiberglass-reinforced plastic (FRP). The tank is partially filled to allow headspace for air.

2. **Frings Aerator (Self-Aspirating Rotor-Stator):** This is the *signature* component. A hollow rotor rotates inside a stator at 1450–1750 rpm. Liquid is drawn up through a central draft tube, accelerated through the rotor-stator gap, and mixed with air sucked in through a venturi port. The system generates **ultra-fine bubbles (< 1 mm diameter)** without an external compressor — a major capital cost saving.

3. **Multiple Impellers** for bulk mixing (Rushton turbines or pitched-blade).

4. **Internal Cooling Coils** or **external jacket** to maintain 28–30 °C (above 32 °C, AAB die).

5. **Inline Acidity Probe** and **automated ethanol feed controller** (in semi-continuous mode).

6. **Loading and Unloading Valves** with sterile air locks.

**Oxygen Transfer — The Rate-Limiting Step:**

Oxygen solubility in vinegar broth (5% acidity) is only ~6–8 mg/L. The Frings aerator overcomes this limitation by achieving **volumetric mass transfer coefficients (kLa) of 400–600 h⁻¹**, an order of magnitude higher than standard sparged tanks. This ensures the bacteria never run out of O₂ even at high cell densities (10⁸ cells/mL).

**Process Operation (Semi-Continuous Fed-Batch Mode):**

1. Tank filled to 60–70% with mash (8–12% v/v ethanol, 4–5% pre-existing acidity).
2. Inoculated with 10% v/v active *Acetobacter* culture.
3. Aeration and agitation begin. Fermentation proceeds for 24–48 h.
4. When acidity reaches 8–10%, **ethanol feed begins** (continuous or pulsed) at a rate that keeps ethanol > 0.2% and acidity < 12%. The fermentation is allowed to run until 10–12% w/v acidity.
5. **Discharge:** 25–40% of the working volume is drawn off; the rest (with cells) is left as **inoculum for the next cycle**. This recycling is what makes the Frings process so efficient.

**Critical Process Parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Temperature | 28 – 30 °C |
| Final acidity | 10 – 15% w/v |
| Fermentation time | 24 – 48 h |
| Aeration rate | 0.1 – 0.3 vvm |
| Agitation | 1450 – 1750 rpm |
| Initial ethanol | 8 – 12% v/v |
| Cell density | 10⁸ cells/mL |

**Why Frings Dominates:**
1. **Speed:** 24–48 h vs 4–8 weeks for Orleans process.
2. **Titer:** 12–15% w/v vs 6–8% for surface methods.
3. **No infection risk:** High acidity (pH 2.4–2.8) excludes contaminating microbes — sterilization of mash is unnecessary.
4. **Continuous operation** with cell recycle — extremely productive.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mark-Loss Zones — KTU 2024 Scheme**
> 1. **Confusing 95.6% azeotrope with absolute alcohol (99.9%).** Many students write "distillation gives 100% ethanol." Wrong — distillation can only reach the azeotrope; dehydration requires molecular sieves. *(-1 mark)*
> 2. **Writing "Lactobacillus converts glucose to lactic acid only" without specifying homo vs hetero.** Always state **homofermentative** when claiming 100% yield. *(-1 mark)*
> 3. **Forgetting the role of O₂ in acetic acid production.** AAB are **strict aerobes** — without O₂, *Acetobacter* cannot oxidize ethanol. Stating "acetic acid is a fermentation product" without mentioning oxidative fermentation is incomplete. *(-1 mark)*
> 4. **Yield calculation: forgetting to multiply by 2 (mol of product).** The ethanol yield is from 2 mol ethanol, not 1. *(-1 mark)*
> 5. **Not labelling the 2 key enzymes (PDC and ADH) in ethanol pathway.** KTU specifically tests enzyme-level understanding. *(-1 mark)*
> 6. **Confusing CaCO₃ role in lactic acid fermentation** — it is a pH neutralizer, not a nutrient. Ca-lactate formed is the precursor to free LA after H₂SO₄ acidification. *(-1 mark)*
> 7. **Acetic acid yield from glucose (incorrect):** Students sometimes use the 0.51 g/g (ethanol) × 1.304 g/g (AA/EtOH) ≈ 0.665 g/g, but this ignores intermediate losses. KTU expects the standard 1.304 g AA / g EtOH formula.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Module 2 — Final Revision Bullets**

### Ethanol
- ✅ Microbe: *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (yeast)
- ✅ Pathway: EMP → Pyruvate → Acetaldehyde (PDC) → Ethanol (ADH)
- ✅ Net: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂
- ✅ Theoretical yield: **0.511 g/g glucose**; practical 90–95%
- ✅ Conditions: 30 °C, pH 4.5, microaerobic
- ✅ End-product inhibition at > 110 g/L ethanol
- ✅ Downstream: Distillation → 95.6% azeotrope → Molecular sieves → 99.9% absolute
- ✅ Major uses: biofuel, beverages, pharma, solvents

### Lactic Acid
- ✅ Microbe: *Lactobacillus* spp. (homofermentative preferred)
- ✅ Pathway: EMP → Pyruvate → Lactic acid (LDH)
- ✅ Net (homo): C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 CH₃CHOHCOOH
- ✅ Theoretical yield: **1.0 g/g glucose** (homo); 0.5 g/g (hetero)
- ✅ Conditions: 37 °C, pH 6.0 (controlled with CaCO₃), anaerobic
- ✅ L(+) isomer preferred for food and PLA
- ✅ Downstream: Ca-lactate precipitation + H₂SO₄ acidification
- ✅ Major uses: food acidulant, PLA bioplastic, pharma, leather

### Acetic Acid
- ✅ Microbe: *Acetobacter aceti* (gram-negative, strict aerobe)
- ✅ Pathway: Ethanol → Acetaldehyde (ADH) → Acetic acid (ALDH)
- ✅ Net: C₂H₅OH + O₂ → CH₃COOH + H₂O
- ✅ Theoretical yield: **1.304 g/g ethanol**
- ✅ Conditions: 28–30 °C, pH 2.4–2.8, strictly aerobic
- ✅ O₂ transfer is the rate-limiting step — Frings aerator solves this
- ✅ Final acidity up to 15% w/v (cell injury above 16%)
- ✅ Downstream: Filtration, aging, pasteurization
- ✅ Major uses: vinegar condiment, industrial chemical, vinyl acetate

### Cross-Comparison Take-Home Points
- ✅ All three are **primary metabolites** — produced in trophophase
- ✅ Ethanol & LA = **fermentative** (anaerobic, substrate-level ATP)
- ✅ Acetic acid = **oxidative** (aerobic, respiratory chain)
- ✅ Yeast = Eukaryote; LAB & AAB = Prokaryotes
- ✅ Theoretical yields: 0.511, 1.000, 1.304 g/g (respectively)
- ✅ KTU loves comparisons — always tabulate parameters side by side

### Numbers to Memorize (Final Day Before Exam)
- Glucose MW = 180.16 g/mol
- Ethanol MW = 46.07 g/mol → ×2 = 92.14
- Lactic acid MW = 90.08 g/mol → ×2 = 180.16
- Acetic acid MW = 60.05 g/mol
- Ethanol azeotrope = 95.6% v/v at 78.2 °C
- Net ATP from EMP = 2 ATP/glucose
- Critical ethanol threshold for yeast = ~110 g/L
- Maximum vinegar acidity without cell death = ~15% w/v
- Frings fermentation time = 24–48 h
- Orleans process time = 4–8 weeks
- % v/v to g/L: multiply by 7.89 (using density 0.789 g/mL)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Microbial Growth and Fermentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT528 - FOOD FERMENTATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Microbial Growth and Fermentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Fermentation Technology (PEFTT528, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term **"Factors Affecting Microbial Growth and Fermentation"** refers to the comprehensive set of intrinsic (substrate-related) and extrinsic (environment-related) parameters that govern the rate, extent, and metabolic direction of microbial proliferation and biochemical conversion within a fermenting food matrix.

As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, these factors are systematically classified into:

1. **Physical Factors** — Temperature, pH, water activity ($a_w$), oxygen availability, redox potential, and surface area.
2. **Chemical / Nutritional Factors** — Carbon source, nitrogen source, minerals, vitamins, and growth promoters/inhibitors.
3. **Biological Factors** — Inoculum concentration, microbial interactions (synergism, antagonism, protocooperation), and contamination.
4. **Process / Fermentation Parameters** — Substrate concentration, agitation, aeration, fermenter geometry, residence time, and product inhibition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to correlate *each factor* with the corresponding **microbial response curve** (lag, log, stationary, decline) and justify the choice of operational conditions for industrial-scale fermenters.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **baking a cake** in your kitchen. The yeast you sprinkle into the dough is the microorganism. Now think of these factors as the "kitchen conditions" that decide whether your cake rises properly:

- **Temperature** → like the oven setting; too cold and the yeast sleeps, too hot and it dies. There is a *Goldilocks zone* (optimum).
- **pH** → like the mood of the dough; acidic environments can excite some microbes and depress others.
- **Water activity ($a_w$)** → like the moisture in the dough; microbes need available water, not just any water.
- **Nutrients (C, N, vitamins)** → like flour, sugar, and milk for the yeast — its food.
- **Oxygen** → some yeasts *want* air (aerobic respiration, like *Candida*), while others *hate* air (anaerobic fermentation, like brewing yeast in a closed bottle of champagne).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Anchor for Exams:** The four letters **"T-P-N-O"** capture the master physical-chemical quartet: **T**emperature, **P** (pH / $a_w$), **N**utrients, **O**xygen. Master these, and you have covered 70 % of Module 2.

---

## 1.3 Standard Microbial Cardinal Points (Memorize)

Every microbial species has three cardinal temperatures:

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum | $T_{min}$ | Lowest temperature permitting growth |
| Optimum | $T_{opt}$ | Temperature of maximum growth rate |
| Maximum | $T_{max}$ | Highest temperature permitting growth |

Based on optimum, microbes are grouped as:

- **Psychrophiles** : $T_{opt} < 15\ ^\circ C$
- **Psychrotrophs** : $T_{opt} \approx 20-30\ ^\circ C$
- **Mesophiles** : $T_{opt} \approx 30-40\ ^\circ C$
- **Thermophiles** : $T_{opt} \approx 55-65\ ^\circ C$
- **Hyperthermophiles** : $T_{opt} > 80\ ^\circ C$

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask: *"Classify the following microorganisms based on their temperature optima and give one industrial application each."* Memorize the table above with examples (e.g., *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus* → thermophile, used in yogurt fermentation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Factors — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Temperature (T)

**Why it matters:** Temperature governs the kinetic energy of molecules and the conformational integrity of microbial enzymes.

**How it operates:**
- ↑ T → ↑ reaction rate (up to $T_{opt}$)
- ↑ T → ↑ rate of protein denaturation (above $T_{max}$)
- Net effect: bell-shaped growth rate curve.

**Operational consequence in fermenters:**
- Mesophilic lactic fermentations: $30-37\ ^\circ C$ (yogurt, idli, dosa batter)
- Thermophilic yogurt cultures: $42-45\ ^\circ C$ (*L. bulgaricus* + *S. thermophilus*)
- Alcoholic fermentation (wine/beer): $15-25\ ^\circ C$ (lager yeasts at lower end)
- Acetic acid fermentation (vinegar): $25-30\ ^\circ C$

### 2.1.2 pH

**Why it matters:** pH affects:
- Ionization state of amino acid side chains in enzymes
- Solubility of CO₂ and other gases
- Permeability of the cell membrane

**How it operates:**
- Each species has its own **pH-cardinal points** ($pH_{min}, pH_{opt}, pH_{max}$).
- Fermentations inherently acidify (organic acid production) — this acts as a *natural biological barrier* against pathogens.

**Industrial Examples:**
- *Lactobacilli* prefer pH 5.5 – 6.5 (sour)
- *Acetobacter* prefers pH 4.0 – 6.0
- *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* prefers pH 4.0 – 5.0

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **yogurt fermentation**, the drop in pH to 4.5 not only curdles casein but also inhibits *Salmonella* and *E. coli*. This dual function makes pH the single most elegant food-safety tool in food biotechnology.

### 2.1.3 Water Activity ($a_w$)

**Definition (formal):**

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

where $p$ is the partial vapor pressure of water in the substrate and $p_0$ is the partial vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature.

**Interpretation:**
- $a_w = 1.0$ → pure water
- $a_w = 0.0$ → completely dry (no available water)
- Most spoilage bacteria require $a_w > 0.91$
- Most spoilage yeasts require $a_w > 0.87$
- Most spoilage molds require $a_w > 0.70$
- *Halobacterium* and *xerotolerant* yeasts can grow at $a_w \approx 0.60$

### 2.1.4 Oxygen and Redox Potential (Eh)

- **Aerobes** require $O_2$ (Eh $\approx +200$ to $+400$ mV)
- **Anaerobes** require absence of $O_2$ (Eh $\approx -200$ mV or below)
- **Facultative anaerobes** (e.g., *S. cerevisiae*) can switch metabolic pathways
- **Microaerophiles** need low $O_2$ partial pressure (2–5 % v/v)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam answers, **always** mention Eh in mV units when classifying anaerobes; it earns an extra mark.

### 2.1.5 Surface Area and Substrate Geometry

Smaller particle size → greater surface-to-volume ratio → ↑ microbial colonization rate. This is why **dosa batter is finely ground** and **idli batter is aerated** before fermentation.

---

## 2.2 Chemical / Nutritional Factors

### 2.2.1 Carbon Source

Acts as the **primary energy source and structural backbone**. The **C:N ratio** is the most critical numerical parameter.

| C:N Ratio | Fermentation Outcome |
|---|---|
| High (>30:1) | Lipid / flavor compound synthesis |
| Moderate (10–20:1) | Biomass + organic acid |
| Low (<5:1) | Poor growth, byproduct accumulation |

### 2.2.2 Nitrogen Source

Provided as **complex** (peptone, yeast extract, malt extract) or **inorganic** ($NH_4^+$, $NO_3^-$) sources. Influences:
- Protein synthesis
- Production of secondary metabolites (e.g., bacteriocins)

### 2.2.3 Growth Factors

- **Vitamins** (B-complex) — *S. cerevisiae* requires biotin, pantothenate, thiamine.
- **Amino acids** — Lactobacilli are "fastidious"; they need 8–10 preformed amino acids.
- **Minerals** — $Mg^{2+}$, $Ca^{2+}$, $Fe^{2+}$, $Zn^{2+}$ act as enzyme cofactors.

### 2.2.4 Inhibitory Compounds

- **Substrate inhibition** (high sugar → *Crabtree effect* in yeasts)
- **Product inhibition** (ethanol, lactic acid accumulation)
- **Spontaneous generation of inhibitory metabolites** (e.g., $H_2O_2$ by *Lactococcus*)

---

## 2.3 Biological Factors

- **Synergism**: *Streptococcus thermophilus* produces formate that *Lactobacillus bulgaricus* uses → mutualistic yogurt culture.
- **Antagonism**: Lactic acid bacteria lower pH → inhibit *Staphylococcus aureus*.
- **Competitiveness**: Mixed cultures compete for nutrients → only the fastest-growing strain dominates.
- **Quorum sensing**: Cell-density-dependent gene regulation — recently implicated in biofilm formation on food-contact surfaces.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In every markdown table below, the absolute value symbol is rendered as $\vert$ (LaTeX vertical bar) to prevent breaking table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units / Limits |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | **Monod growth kinetics**; $\mu$ = specific growth rate, $\mu_{max}$ = maximum specific growth rate, $S$ = substrate concentration, $K_s$ = half-saturation constant | $\mu$ in $h^{-1}$; $K_s$ in $g \cdot L^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ | **Doubling (generation) time** | hours |
| 3 | $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$ | Exponential phase biomass accumulation; $N_0$ = initial cell density | cells $\cdot mL^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu X$ | Differential form of growth in batch culture | $X$ = biomass conc. ($g \cdot L^{-1}$) |
| 5 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | **Arrhenius equation** for temperature dependence of microbial rate | $E_a$ in $J \cdot mol^{-1}$, $R = 8.314\ J \cdot mol^{-1} K^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $Q_{10} = \left( \dfrac{k_{T+10}}{k_T} \right)^{10 / \Delta T}$ | Temperature coefficient of reaction rate | Dimensionless, $\approx 2-3$ for most reactions |
| 7 | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Water activity | Dimensionless, $0 \le a_w \le 1$ |
| 8 | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$ | Growth yield coefficient | $g$ biomass per $g$ substrate |
| 9 | $P = P_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_p + S} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{P}{P_{crit}}\right)$ | **Luedeking-Piret equation** simplified form for product formation; $P$ = product conc., $K_p$ = product half-sat., $P_{crit}$ = product inhibition constant | $P$ in $g \cdot L^{-1}$ |
| 10 | $k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = OTR$ | Oxygen transfer rate; $C^*$ = saturation conc., $C_L$ = dissolved $O_2$ conc. | $mol \cdot L^{-1} \cdot h^{-1}$ |
| 11 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{F}$ | **Residence time** in CSTR / continuous fermenter; $V$ = volume, $F$ = flow rate | hours |
| 12 | $D = \mu$ (at steady state) | **Dilution rate equals growth rate** in chemostat; washout when $D > \mu_{max}$ | $h^{-1}$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

| Industry Application | Critical Factor | Engineering Decision |
|---|---|---|
| Yogurt production | Temperature + pH | Jacketed fermenter at $42\ ^\circ C$ with pH-stat at 4.5 |
| Wine fermentation | $O_2$ + temperature | Closed stainless tank at $18-22\ ^\circ C$ |
| Idli/dosa batter | Inoculum (back-slopping) | *L. mesenteroides* carried over from previous batch |
| Sourdough bread | pH + organic acids | Long fermentation ($\approx 8$ h) develops flavor via acetic/lactic acid balance |
| Tempeh | Aeration | Shallow trays, periodic flipping, since *Rhizopus oligosporus* is aerobic |
| Vinegar | Continuous aeration | Frings acetator supplies $\approx 0.1\ vvm$ air |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Monod Growth Kinetics

The Monod equation is the **most important kinetic model** in fermentation technology. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Start with the mass-balance differential equation for biomass in a batch reactor.

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X - k_d \cdot X$$

where $X$ is biomass concentration, $\mu$ is the specific growth rate, and $k_d$ is the endogenous decay constant.

For the **exponential phase** (decay is negligible in the active growth window), we set $k_d \approx 0$:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X$$

### Step 2 — Monod's empirical observation.

Jacques Monod (1942) observed that the specific growth rate $\mu$ varies with limiting substrate $S$ following a **hyperbolic saturation curve** analogous to Michaelis-Menten enzyme kinetics:

$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$

**Reasoning for the form:** At very low $S$, $K_s + S \approx K_s$, so $\mu \approx (\mu_{max} / K_s) \cdot S$ (first-order in $S$). At very high $S$, $K_s + S \approx S$, so $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$ (zero-order saturation).

### Step 3 — Combine the two relations.

Substituting Monod's expression into the differential equation:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} \cdot X$$

### Step 4 — Substrate consumption link.

In a batch reactor, biomass production is linked to substrate consumption via the yield coefficient $Y_{X/S}$:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$

Rearranging and integrating gives the **logistic-like trajectory** used in process design.

### Step 5 — Compute the doubling time.

In the exponential phase where $S \gg K_s$, we have $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$. Then:

$$X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu_{max} \cdot t}$$

For one doubling, $X(t_d) = 2 X_0$:

$$2 X_0 = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu_{max} \cdot t_d}$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln 2 = \mu_{max} \cdot t_d$$

Hence:

$$t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu_{max}} = \frac{0.693}{\mu_{max}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU typical)

> A starter culture has $\mu_{max} = 0.42\ h^{-1}$ in exponential phase. Calculate the doubling time and the biomass after $6$ hours if $X_0 = 1 \times 10^5\ CFU \cdot mL^{-1}$.

**Step (i) — Doubling time:**

$$t_d = \frac{0.693}{0.42} = 1.65\ h$$

**Step (ii) — Number of doublings in 6 h:**

$$n = \frac{6}{1.65} = 3.64$$

**Step (iii) — Final biomass:**

$$X_6 = 1 \times 10^5 \times 2^{3.64} = 1 \times 10^5 \times 12.46 = 1.246 \times 10^6\ CFU \cdot mL^{-1}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Q₁₀ Temperature Coefficient

The Q₁₀ rule states that for every $10\ ^\circ C$ rise (within the biological range), the reaction rate approximately doubles.

### Step 1 — Start from Arrhenius.

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

### Step 2 — Take the ratio of rates at $T + 10$ and $T$:

$$Q_{10} = \frac{k_{T+10}}{k_T} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a / R(T+10)}}{A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}}$$

The pre-exponential $A$ cancels:

$$Q_{10} = e^{E_a / R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T+10}\right)}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the bracket.

For any temperature difference $\Delta T$:

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T + \Delta T} = \frac{\Delta T}{T(T + \Delta T)}$$

For $\Delta T = 10$ K around $T = 310\ K$ (≈ $37\ ^\circ C$):

$$\frac{1}{310} - \frac{1}{320} = \frac{10}{310 \times 320} = \frac{10}{99200} = 1.008 \times 10^{-4}\ K^{-1}$$

### Step 4 — Compute $E_a$ when $Q_{10} = 2$:

$$2 = e^{E_a \cdot 1.008 \times 10^{-4} / R}$$

Take natural log:

$$\ln 2 = \frac{E_a \cdot 1.008 \times 10^{-4}}{8.314}$$

$$0.693 = E_a \cdot 1.212 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$E_a = \frac{0.693}{1.212 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 57{,}180\ J \cdot mol^{-1} \approx 57.2\ kJ \cdot mol^{-1}$$

This is a typical activation energy for microbial growth, validating the $Q_{10} \approx 2$ rule.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Factor on Microbial Kinetics

| Factor | Effect on $\mu_{max}$ | Effect on $K_s$ | Effect on $Y_{X/S}$ | Effect on Product Profile | Industrial Control Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature (up to $T_{opt}$) | ↑↑ increases | ≈ no change | ≈ unchanged | Shifts to byproducts | Jacketed vessel with coolant |
| ↑ pH (up to $pH_{opt}$) | ↑ | ↓ (better affinity) | ↑ | Cleaner primary product | pH-stat with $NaOH / NH_4OH$ |
| ↑ $a_w$ | ↑ (up to limit) | ↓ | ↑ | Higher acid yield | Salt/sugar addition |
| ↑ Dissolved $O_2$ | ↑ for aerobes | ↑ | ↑ for aerobes | ↑ biomass | Sparger + impeller |
| ↑ Substrate $S$ | Saturated above $\approx 10 K_s$ | — | ↓ at very high $S$ (Crabtree) | ↑ ethanol at high $S$ | Fed-batch strategy |
| ↑ Inoculum size | ↓ lag time | — | — | Faster pH drop | Pre-culture standardization |
| ↑ Agitation (rpm) | ↑ up to a point | — | ↑ (better $k_L a$) | Better mass transfer | Rushton / pitched-blade turbines |
| ↑ Product concentration | ↓ (inhibition) | — | ↓ | Self-limiting | In-situ product removal (ISPR) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation: Critical Dilution Rate and Washout in a Chemostat

In a steady-state continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR):

**Step 1 — Mass balance on biomass:**

$$\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Growth} = \text{Accumulation}$$

$$0 - D \cdot X + \mu \cdot X = 0$$

where $D = F / V$ is the dilution rate.

**Step 2 — Solve:**

$$D = \mu$$

**Step 3 — Apply Monod:**

$$D = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$

**Step 4 — Critical dilution $D_c$ at washout** (where $S \to S_{in}$, the inlet substrate):

$$D_c = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}}$$

**Worked example:** If $\mu_{max} = 0.5\ h^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.2\ g \cdot L^{-1}$, $S_{in} = 10\ g \cdot L^{-1}$:

$$D_c = 0.5 \cdot \frac{10}{0.2 + 10} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{10}{10.2} = 0.490\ h^{-1}$$

Operation above $D = 0.490\ h^{-1}$ causes **washout** — the entire microbial population is flushed out of the vessel. KTU exams love this calculation; commit it to memory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Factors Affecting Microbial Growth

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph EXT[EXTRINSIC FACTORS]
        nodeA[Temperature T]
        nodeB[pH]
        nodeC[Water Activity aw]
        nodeD[Dissolved O2 and Redox Eh]
        nodeE[Agitation rpm]
    end

    subgraph INT[INTRINSIC FACTORS]
        nodeF[Carbon Source C]
        nodeG[Nitrogen Source N]
        nodeH[Vitamins and Minerals]
        nodeI[C to N Ratio]
    end

    subgraph BIO[BIOLOGICAL FACTORS]
        nodeJ[Inoculum Size]
        nodeK[Microbial Interactions]
        nodeL[Quorum Sensing]
    end

    subgraph PROC[PROCESS FACTORS]
        nodeM[Substrate Concentration S]
        nodeN[Fermentation Time t]
        nodeO[Residence Time tau]
    end

    nodeA --> RESP[Microbial Growth Response]
    nodeB --> RESP
    nodeC --> RESP
    nodeD --> RESP
    nodeE --> RESP
    nodeF --> RESP
    nodeG --> RESP
    nodeH --> RESP
    nodeI --> RESP
    nodeJ --> RESP
    nodeK --> RESP
    nodeL --> RESP
    nodeM --> RESP
    nodeN --> RESP
    nodeO --> RESP

    RESP --> KIN[Monod Kinetics mu]
    RESP --> YLD[Yield Coefficient YXS]
    RESP --> PROD[Product Profile P]

    KIN --> OUT[Final Fermented Food Quality]
    YLD --> OUT
    PROD --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Microbial Growth Curve (Lag → Log → Stationary → Decline) with Overlaid Factor Influence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inoculation] --> B[Lag Phase]
    B --> C[Log Phase Exponential]
    C --> D[Stationary Phase]
    D --> E[Decline Phase]

    B -. Affected by .-> F1[Inoculum size and pre-adaptation]
    C -. Affected by .-> F2[Nutrients Temperature pH dissolved O2]
    D -. Affected by .-> F3[Nutrient depletion and product inhibition]
    E -. Affected by .-> F4[aw pH ethanol lactic acid]

    style B fill:#fff4cc
    style C fill:#cce5ff
    style D fill:#d4edda
    style E fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.3 Fermenter Control Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP[Set Point pH 4.5 and T 42 C] --> CTRL[PID Controller]
    CTRL --> ACT[Actuator: Acid or Base Pump and Cooling Coil Valve]
    ACT --> VESSEL[Fermenter Vessel]
    VESSEL --> SENS1[pH Probe]
    VESSEL --> SENS2[Temperature Sensor]
    VESSEL --> SENS3[Dissolved O2 Probe]
    SENS1 --> CTRL
    SENS2 --> CTRL
    SENS3 --> CTRL
    VESSEL --> PROD[Outlet Product Stream]

    style CTRL fill:#ffe4b5
    style VESSEL fill:#add8e6
    style PROD fill:#90ee90
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting Fermentation Conditions for a New Product

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the target organism aerobic?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Use sparged aerated CSTR]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is it facultative?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Closed vessel with limited headspace]
    Q2 -- No --> A3[Strictly anaerobic with N2 sparging]
    A1 --> Q3{Optimum pH acidic or neutral?}
    A2 --> Q3
    A3 --> Q3
    Q3 -- Acidic --> A4[pH 3.5 to 5.5 control with pH stat]
    Q3 -- Neutral --> A5[pH 6.5 to 7.5 with phosphate buffer]
    A4 --> Q4{Product is biomass or metabolite?}
    A5 --> Q4
    Q4 -- Biomass --> OUT1[Short residence time high yield]
    Q4 -- Metabolite --> OUT2[Long residence time fed batch]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagrammatic Reading Strategy for KTU:** When a question gives a 14-mark block diagram of a fermenter, label **all 12 standard ports** (inoculum, air, media, sampling, harvest, exhaust, CIP, SIP, instrument ports). Missing port labels is the most common reason for the 1–2 mark deduction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define water activity ($a_w$) and state the minimum $a_w$ values required for the growth of bacteria, yeasts, and molds. [3 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Water activity is defined as the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a substrate ($p$) to the partial vapor pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity that represents the *available* (unbound) water in a food system, which microorganisms can use for metabolic activity.

**Minimum $a_w$ thresholds for microbial growth:**

- **Bacteria:** $a_w \ge 0.91$ (most species); halophilic bacteria as low as $0.75$.
- **Yeasts:** $a_w \ge 0.87$ (most); xerotolerant yeasts as low as $0.60$.
- **Molds:** $a_w \ge 0.70$ (most); xerophilic molds as low as $0.61$.

**Significance in food preservation:** Reducing $a_w$ below these thresholds (by adding salt, sugar, or drying) is a key hurdle in controlling spoilage organisms and enhancing shelf-life of fermented and preserved foods.

---

### Q2. List the four phases of a microbial growth curve and write the differential equation governing the exponential (log) phase. [3 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The four phases of a typical batch microbial growth curve are:

1. **Lag Phase** — cells adapt to new environment; no net increase in cell number.
2. **Log (Exponential) Phase** — cells divide at a constant maximum specific growth rate.
3. **Stationary Phase** — growth rate equals death rate due to nutrient depletion and/or product accumulation.
4. **Decline (Death) Phase** — cells die faster than they multiply.

**Differential equation for the log phase:**

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max} \cdot X$$

where $X$ is the biomass concentration and $\mu_{max}$ is the maximum specific growth rate (units: $h^{-1}$).

The integrated form gives biomass as an exponential function of time:

$$X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu_{max} \cdot t}$$

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEFTT528 Module 2]**

**(a)** Classify the major factors affecting microbial growth in food fermentation. Discuss the role of **temperature and pH** in detail with suitable industrial examples. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Derive the **Monod growth equation** starting from a biomass mass balance in a batch reactor. A starter culture of *Lactobacillus plantarum* has $\mu_{max} = 0.38\ h^{-1}$ and $K_s = 0.5\ g \cdot L^{-1}$.
