topics.title: Fermented Fruits and Vegetable Products: Sauerkraut, Kimchi, Pickles - Lactic Acid Bacteria and Fermentation Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT528 - FOOD FERMENTATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fermented Fruits and Vegetable Products: Sauerkraut, Kimchi, Pickles - Lactic Acid Bacteria and Fermentation Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fermented Fruit and Vegetable Products — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fermented Fruits and Vegetable Products** are food products derived from fresh horticultural commodities (cabbage, cucumber, radish, olives, carrots, etc.) that have undergone a controlled microbial conversion, primarily mediated by **Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB)**, resulting in characteristic sour taste, extended shelf-life, improved nutritional profile, and unique organoleptic properties through the production of lactic acid, CO₂, ethanol, acetic acid, and various flavour-active compounds.

The fermentation of vegetables is fundamentally an **anaerobic / microaerophilic bioprocess** where naturally present or starter-inoculated LAB metabolize available carbohydrates (mainly glucose, fructose, sucrose) into organic acids (predominantly **lactic acid**), thereby:
- Lowering the **pH** below 4.5 (inhibiting spoilage and pathogenic microbes)
- Producing **antimicrobial compounds** (bacteriocins)
- Creating unique **flavour and texture** profiles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB)** are Gram-positive, non-spore forming, catalase-negative, aerotolerant anaerobes belonging to the phylum *Firmicutes*. The most industrially relevant genera are *Lactobacillus*, *Lactococcus*, *Leuconostoc*, *Pediococcus*, and *Streptococcus*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Natural Pickling Jar"**
> Imagine placing fresh cabbage in a sealed jar with salt. The salt draws out moisture via **osmosis**, creating a brine that submerges the vegetable. This brine, now devoid of oxygen, becomes a **private swimming pool for beneficial bacteria** (LAB) that were always hiding on the cabbage leaves. These "good tenants" consume the natural sugars and excrete lactic acid, transforming the bland cabbage into tangy sauerkraut. Spoilage bacteria, in contrast, are like unwanted guests — the acidic environment acts as a strict bouncer, throwing them out.

In essence, **fermentation is a controlled microbial "renovation" of food** — preserving nutrients, enhancing digestibility, and producing bioactives such as vitamins, gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), and bioactive peptides.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard Fermentation Parameters for Major Vegetable Fermentations:
> - **Salt Concentration (Brine):** **2.0 – 2.5 % (w/v)** for sauerkraut; **5 – 8 %** for kimchi; **5 – 10 %** for cucumber pickles
> - **Final pH:** **3.4 – 3.6** (sauerkraut), **3.8 – 4.2** (kimchi), **3.5 – 5.0** (pickles)
> - **Titratable Acidity (as lactic acid):** **0.8 – 1.5 %**
> - **Fermentation Temperature:** **18 – 22 °C** (sauerkraut), **4 – 10 °C** (kimchi), **20 – 30 °C** (pickles)
> - **Anaerobic condition** maintained by submerging vegetables below brine surface

## 1.4 Classification of Lactic Acid Bacteria

LAB are classified into two metabolic groups based on their fermentation pathway:

| **Group** | **Metabolic Type** | **End Products** | **Representative Genera** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Homofermentative** | EMP Pathway (Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas) | **> 85 %** Lactic Acid | *Lactobacillus plantarum, L. acidophilus, L. delbrueckii, Pediococcus cerevisiae, Lactococcus lactis* |
| **Heterofermentative** | Phosphoketolase (6-P-Gluconate) Pathway | Lactic Acid + CO₂ + Ethanol/Acetic Acid | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides, Lactobacillus brevis, L. fermentum* |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biochemistry of Lactic Acid Fermentation

### 2.1.1 Homofermentative Pathway (EMP Pathway)

> [!NOTE]
> **EMP / Glycolytic Pathway** — Used by homofermentative LAB. One mole of glucose yields **2 moles of lactic acid** and a net gain of **2 ATP** via substrate-level phosphorylation.

**Overall Stoichiometric Equation:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{EMP Pathway}} 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + 2\,\text{ATP}
$$

**Net Energy Yield:** **2 ATP per glucose molecule**.

### 2.1.2 Heterofermentative Pathway (Phosphoketolase Pathway)

> [!NOTE]
> **Phosphoketolase Pathway** — Used by heterofermentative LAB. Glucose is partially oxidised via the pentose phosphate pathway. One mole of glucose yields **1 mole each of lactic acid, ethanol (or acetic acid), and CO₂** with a net gain of **1 ATP**.

**Overall Stoichiometric Equation:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{6-P-Gluconate Pathway}} \text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{ATP}
$$

**Net Energy Yield:** **1 ATP per glucose molecule**.

## 2.2 Microbial Succession During Vegetable Fermentation

A typical spontaneous vegetable fermentation follows a **defined microbial succession**:

| **Phase** | **Dominant Microorganism(s)** | **Metabolic Role** | **Duration** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1 — Initiation** | Gram-negative bacteria (*Enterobacter, Erwinia, Flavobacterium*) | Begin utilising sugars, consume residual O₂ | **0 – 24 h** |
| **Phase 2 — Early Acidification** | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* (heterofermentative) | Produces lactic acid, CO₂, ethanol, mannitol; creates anaerobic conditions | **1 – 3 days** |
| **Phase 3 — Main Acidification** | *Lactobacillus plantarum, L. brevis, Pediococcus* | Rapid lactic acid production; pH drops to **< 4.5** | **3 – 14 days** |
| **Phase 4 — Final / Stabilisation** | *Lactobacillus plantarum, L. sakei* | Continued acidification to pH 3.4 – 3.6; flavour maturation | **14 – 30+ days** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations and parameters are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 examinations. Memorise the stoichiometry and pH/salt ranges.

| **Concept** | **Equation / Expression** | **Units / Standard Value** |
|---|---|---|
| **Lactic Acid Production (Homo)** | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3$ | mol/mol basis |
| **Lactic Acid Production (Hetero)** | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$ | mol/mol basis |
| **ATP Yield (Homo)** | $Y_{ATP} = 2\,\text{mol ATP} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}\,\text{glucose}$ | dimensionless |
| **ATP Yield (Hetero)** | $Y_{ATP} = 1\,\text{mol ATP} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}\,\text{glucose}$ | dimensionless |
| **Titratable Acidity** | $TA = \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 0.09 \times 100}{V_{sample}}$ | % lactic acid |
| **pH Drop (Homo Acidification)** | $\Delta pH = pH_{initial} - pH_{final} \approx 6.0 - 3.5 = 2.5$ | pH units |
| **Salt Brine Concentration** | $C_{brine} = \frac{m_{salt}}{V_{water} + m_{salt}} \times 100$ | % (w/v) |
| **Water Activity Reduction** | $a_w \approx 1 - 0.005 \times C_{salt}$ | dimensionless (for NaCl) |
| **Henderson-Hasselbalch (Lactate buffer)** | $pH = pK_a + \log\frac{\vert \text{Lactate}^- \vert}{\vert \text{Lactic Acid} \vert}$ | pH units; $pK_a = 3.86$ |
| **Optical Density (Growth)** | $OD_{600nm} = k \cdot N_{cells}$ | cells/mL (via calibration) |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Note:** In mathematical expressions inside the table, the **vertical bar** used for absolute value / concentration (e.g., $\vert \text{Lactate}^- \vert$) is written as `\vert` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. Do **not** use a raw pipe character $\mid$ inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The principles of LAB fermentation are applied across multiple industries:
> - **Food Preservation Industry:** Industrial-scale production of sauerkraut, kimchi, table olives, and cucumber pickles.
> - **Probiotic Foods:** Yogurt, kefir, kombucha, and fermented vegetable juices (functional foods).
> - **Biotechnology:** Production of **bacteriocins** (e.g., nisin from *Lactococcus lactis*) used as **natural bio-preservatives** (E234).
> - **Bioenergy & Biorefinery:** LAB for **lactic acid production** — a key platform chemical for **polylactic acid (PLA) bioplastics**.
> - **Fermented Plant-Based Meat:** LAB-mediated acidification for texture and flavour development in plant protein products.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lactic Acid Stoichiometry from Glucose (Homofermentative)

We derive the stoichiometry of homofermentative lactic acid fermentation step-by-step from the EMP pathway.

**Step 1 — Phosphorylation of Glucose (Hexokinase Reaction):**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{ATP} \rightarrow \text{Glucose-6-P} + \text{ADP}
$$

**Step 2 — Isomerisation (Phosphoglucose Isomerase):**

$$
\text{Glucose-6-P} \rightarrow \text{Fructose-6-P}
$$

**Step 3 — Second Phosphorylation (PFK-1):**

$$
\text{Fructose-6-P} + \text{ATP} \rightarrow \text{Fructose-1,6-bisP} + \text{ADP}
$$

**Step 4 — Aldolase Cleavage (6-C → 2 × 3-C):**

$$
\text{Fructose-1,6-bisP} \rightarrow \text{DHAP} + \text{Glyceraldehyde-3-P}
$$

**Step 5 — DHAP ⇌ G3P (Triosephosphate Isomerase):**

$$
\text{DHAP} \rightarrow \text{Glyceraldehyde-3-P} \quad (\text{2 G3P per glucose})
$$

**Step 6 — GAPDH Phosphorylation & Oxidation:**

$$
\text{G3P} + \text{NAD}^+ + \text{P}_i \rightarrow \text{1,3-Bisphosphoglycerate} + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+
$$

**Step 7 — Substrate-Level Phosphorylation (PGK):**

$$
\text{1,3-BPG} + \text{ADP} \rightarrow \text{3-Phosphoglycerate} + \text{ATP}
$$

**Step 8 — Phosphoglycerate Mutase & Enolase Steps:**

$$
\text{3-PG} \rightarrow \text{2-PG} \rightarrow \text{Phosphoenolpyruvate} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 9 — Pyruvate Kinase (Final ATP):**

$$
\text{PEP} + \text{ADP} \rightarrow \text{Pyruvate} + \text{ATP}
$$

**Step 10 — Lactate Dehydrogenase (Lactic Acid Formation):**

$$
\text{Pyruvate} + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Lactate} + \text{NAD}^+
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Result:** For each glucose molecule, **2 ATP are consumed** (Steps 1 & 3) and **4 ATP are produced** (Steps 7 & 9, ×2 for the 2 trioses). Net ATP gain = 4 − 2 = **2 ATP**. Lactic acid produced = **2 mol per mol glucose**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Equation (Homofermentative):} \\
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 2\,\text{ADP} + 2\,\text{P}_i \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3 + 2\,\text{ATP} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Heterofermentative Phosphoketolase Pathway

> [!NOTE]
> Heterofermentative LAB lack the key EMP enzyme **fructose-1,6-bisphosphate aldolase**, forcing glucose metabolism through the **pentose phosphate / phosphoketolase route**.

**Step 1 — Glucose Oxidation to 6-Phosphogluconate:**

$$
\text{Glucose} + \text{ATP} \rightarrow \text{Glucose-6-P} \rightarrow \text{6-Phosphogluconate} + \text{NADPH}
$$

**Step 2 — Decarboxylation (6-P-Gluconate Dehydratase):**

$$
\text{6-P-Gluconate} \rightarrow \text{Ribulose-5-P} + \text{CO}_2
$$

**Step 3 — Phosphoketolase (Key Cleavage):**

$$
\text{Ribulose-5-P} + \text{P}_i \rightarrow \text{G3P} + \text{Acetyl-P}
$$

**Step 4 — G3P → Pyruvate (via partial EMP, yielding 1 ATP):**

$$
\text{G3P} \rightarrow \text{Pyruvate} \rightarrow \text{Lactate} \quad (\text{1 ATP formed})
$$

**Step 5 — Acetyl-Phosphate Conversion:**

$$
\text{Acetyl-P} + \text{ADP} \rightarrow \text{Acetate} + \text{ATP} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Acetaldehyde} \rightarrow \text{Ethanol}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Result:** Glucose is split into G3P (yielding 1 lactate + 1 ATP) and Acetyl-P (yielding ethanol/acetate + 1 ATP). However, since the NADPH generated in Step 1 is consumed in reducing acetyl-P to ethanol, only **1 net ATP** is produced per glucose.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Equation (Heterofermentative):} \\
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{ADP} + \text{P}_i \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{ATP}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Three Fermented Products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This analytical matrix is a **guaranteed KTU exam question** in some form. Master the comparison.

| **Parameter** | **Sauerkraut** | **Kimchi** | **Pickles (Cucumber)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Raw Material** | Cabbage (*Brassica oleracea*) | Napa cabbage, radish, mixed vegetables | Cucumbers (*Cucumis sativus*) |
| **Origin** | Germany / Eastern Europe | Korea | India / Middle East (cucumber variants) |
| **Salt Concentration (Brine)** | 2.0 – 2.5 % | 5 – 8 % | 5 – 10 % |
| **Spices / Additives** | None (pure lactic fermentation) | Garlic, ginger, red pepper (*gochugaru*), fish sauce, glutinous rice paste | Dill, garlic, peppercorns, bay leaves (flavour profile) |
| **Dominant LAB (Early)** | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*, *W. koreensis* | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* |
| **Dominant LAB (Late)** | *Lactobacillus plantarum, L. brevis* | *L. plantarum, L. sakei* | *L. plantarum, L. brevis, P. cerevisiae* |
| **Final pH** | 3.4 – 3.6 | 3.8 – 4.2 | 3.5 – 5.0 (depending on style) |
| **Storage** | Refrigerated / canned | Cold storage (kimchi refrigerators at ~0 °C) | Refrigerated brined or pasteurised |
| **Shelf Life** | 6 – 12 months | 3 – 6 months (fresh kimchi) | 1 – 2 years (pasteurised) |
| **Special Feature** | Homolactic finish; clean sour taste | Complex flavour due to spices & seafood; multiple sub-varieties | Often consumed as fresh-pack / refrigerated |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Manufacturing Process — Sauerkraut

**Stage 1 — Raw Material Selection & Preparation:**
- Fresh, mature cabbage heads are trimmed, cored, and shredded (2 – 3 mm).
- Damaged outer leaves removed; trimmed heads washed.

**Stage 2 — Salting & Tamping:**
- Salt (2.25 % w/w of cabbage mass) is mixed uniformly with shredded cabbage.
- Salt draws out cellular juice via **osmosis**; cabbage is tamped into fermentation tanks to expel air and form natural brine.

**Stage 3 — Spontaneous Fermentation (4 Phases):**
- *Phase I (0 – 24 h):* Mixed flora, predominantly Gram-negative.
- *Phase II (1 – 3 days):* *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* dominance, CO₂ production, slight effervescence.
- *Phase III (3 – 14 days):* *Lactobacillus* species rapidly acidify; pH < 4.0.
- *Phase IV (14 – 30+ days):* Final maturation; homofermentative finish by *L. plantarum*.

**Stage 4 — Packaging & Storage:**
- Sauerkraut is hot-filled at 75 – 80 °C into cans/jars, vacuum-sealed, and pasteurised to inactivate LAB and prevent post-packaging fermentation.
- Alternatively, refrigerated for fresh-pack sauerkraut.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Manufacturing Process — Kimchi

**Stage 1 — Brining of Napa Cabbage (Kimjang Process):**
- Quartered cabbage is brined in **10 – 15 % salt water** for 8 – 12 hours until leaves become pliable.
- Brined cabbage is rinsed thoroughly to remove excess salt.

**Stage 2 — Preparation of Yangnyeom (Seasoning Paste):**
- A spice paste is prepared from **garlic, ginger, gochugaru (red pepper), fish sauce (myeolchi-aekjeot), glutinous rice paste (jorim), and radish cubes**.
- This paste is rubbed uniformly between cabbage leaves.

**Stage 3 — Fermentation:**
- Packaged tightly in airtight containers (onggi) and fermented at **4 – 10 °C** (modern refrigerators) or ambient (traditional).
- Fermentation duration: 1 day to several weeks depending on desired sourness.

**Stage 4 — Storage:**
- Stored at **−1 to 0 °C** in specialised kimchi refrigerators to maintain crispness and slow over-acidification.

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Manufacturing Process — Cucumber Pickles

**Stage 1 — Grading & Brining:**
- Fresh cucumbers (size 3 – 8 cm) are washed, graded, and brined with **5 – 8 % NaCl**.
- Calcium chloride (CaCl₂, 0.1 – 0.2 %) is often added for **texture firming** via pectate crosslinking.

**Stage 2 — Fermentation:**
- *Phase I (0 – 2 days):* Aerobic Gram-negative bacteria; brine turns turbid.
- *Phase II (2 – 7 days):* *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* dominates; produces lactic acid, CO₂, ethanol.
- *Phase III (7 – 14 days):* *Lactobacillus plantarum* and *Pediococcus cerevisiae* complete acidification; pH stabilises at 3.5 – 4.0.

**Stage 3 — Post-Fermentation Processing:**
- Pasteurisation at **74 °C / 15 min** for shelf-stable pickles.
- Refrigerated storage for fresh-packed dills.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Microbial Succession in Vegetable Fermentation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fresh Vegetable Harvest] --> B[Cleaning & Trimming]
    B --> C[Shredding / Brining with NaCl 2-10%]
    C --> D[Anaerobic Tank / Jar Sealing]
    D --> E["Phase 1: Initiation (0-24h)"]
    E --> F1["Gram-negative bacteria: Enterobacter, Erwinia"]
    F1 --> G["Phase 2: Early Acidification (1-3 days)"]
    G --> F2["Leuconostoc mesenteroides"]
    F2 --> H["Phase 3: Main Acidification (3-14 days)"]
    H --> F3["Lactobacillus plantarum and Pediococcus"]
    F3 --> I["Phase 4: Final Stabilisation (14-30+ days)"]
    I --> F4["Lactobacillus sakei and L. plantarum"]
    F4 --> J["Final Product: pH 3.4-4.2, TA 0.8-1.5%"]
    J --> K[Packaging and Storage]
    K --> L{Pasteurisation Required?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Shelf-Stable Product]
    L -- No --> N[Refrigerated Fresh Pack]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Comparative Block Architecture of the Three Fermentation Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        S1[Cabbage Shredding] --> S2[2.25% NaCl Salting]
        S2 --> S3[Tamping and Brine Formation]

        S4 --> S5[Hot Fill at 75-80 C]
        S5 --> S6[Sauerkraut Final Product]
    end
    
    subgraph KIM[KIMCHI PROCESS]
        K1[Napa Cabbage Halving] --> K2[10-15% Brine Soak 8-12h]
        K2 --> K3[Rinse and Drain]
        K3 --> K4[Apply Yangnyeom Spice Paste]
        K4 --> K5[4-10 C Fermentation 1-21 days]
        K5 --> K6[Storage at 0 C]
        K6 --> K7[Kimchi Final Product]
    end
    
    subgraph PIC[PICKLE PROCESS]
        P1[Cucumber Grading and Washing] --> P2[5-8% NaCl plus CaCl2 Brine]
        P2 --> P3[20-30 C Fermentation 7-14 days]
        P3 --> P4[Pasteurisation 74 C 15 min]
        P4 --> P5[Pickle Final Product]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Biochemical Pathway Decision Tree (LAB Metabolism)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START([Glucose Substrate]) --> Q1{Is aldolase present?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> EMP[EMP Glycolytic Pathway]
    Q1 -- No --> PPP[Phosphoketolase Pathway]
    EMP --> E1[Pyruvate via GAPDH and PK Steps]
    E1 --> E2[Lactate Dehydrogenase]
    E2 --> E3["Product: 2 Lactic Acid + 2 ATP"]
    PPP --> P1[6-Phosphogluconate Formation]
    P1 --> P2[Ribulose-5-P plus CO2]
    P2 --> P3[Phosphoketolase Cleavage]
    P3 --> P4[G3P and Acetyl Phosphate]
    P4 --> P5[G3P to Lactate: 1 ATP]
    P4 --> P6[Acetyl Phosphate to Ethanol or Acetate]
    P5 --> P7["Product: 1 Lactic Acid"]
    P6 --> P8["Product: 1 Ethanol and 1 CO2"]
    P7 --> FINAL[Net: 1 Lactate + 1 Ethanol + 1 CO2 + 1 ATP]
    P8 --> FINAL
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB). Differentiate between homofermentative and heterofermentative LAB with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB) are Gram-positive, non-spore forming, catalase-negative, aerotolerant anaerobic bacteria that primarily ferment carbohydrates into lactic acid. They belong to the phylum *Firmicutes*, with industrially important genera being *Lactobacillus*, *Lactococcus*, *Leuconostoc*, *Pediococcus*, and *Streptococcus*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Differentiation (2 Marks):**

| **Feature** | **Homofermentative LAB** | **Heterofermentative LAB** |
|---|---|---|
| **Pathway** | EMP (Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas) | Phosphoketolase (6-P-Gluconate) |
| **End Products** | ≥ 85 % Lactic Acid | Lactic Acid + CO₂ + Ethanol/Acetic Acid |
| **ATP Yield** | 2 ATP / glucose | 1 ATP / glucose |
| **Examples** | *Lactobacillus plantarum, L. delbrueckii, Pediococcus cerevisiae* | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides, L. brevis* |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six important fermented vegetable products. Explain the role of salt in vegetable fermentation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Fermented Vegetable Products (1.5 Marks):**
> 1. Sauerkraut (fermented cabbage)
> 2. Kimchi (Korean fermented vegetables)
> 3. Cucumber pickles
> 4. Table olives
> 5. Sour pickles / Indian achaar
> 6. Fermented carrots / turnips / radish

> [!NOTE]
> **Role of Salt (1.5 Marks):**
> 1. **Selective Inhibitor:** Suppresses Gram-negative spoilage bacteria (e.g., *Enterobacter*, *Erwinia*) while favouring LAB growth.
> 2. **Osmotic Effect:** Draws out cellular juice via osmosis, forming natural brine for **submerged anaerobic fermentation**.
> 3. **Texture Improvement:** Maintains crispness of vegetables by strengthening pectic structure.
> 4. **Flavour Development:** Contributes to characteristic salty taste and aids flavour compound extraction.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Describe in detail the manufacturing process of sauerkraut. Explain the microbial succession, biochemistry of fermentation, and quality parameters of the final product.**

#### **Part (a) — Manufacturing Process & Microbial Succession (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Raw Material Selection (1 Mark):**
Fresh, mature white cabbage (*Brassica oleracea var. capitata*) with solid heads, no insect damage, and high sugar content (4 – 6 %) is selected. Outer green leaves are removed; core is excised.

**Step 2 — Shredding and Salting (1.5 Marks):**
Cabbage is shredded into 2 – 3 mm strips using mechanical shredders. Salt (NaCl) is added at **2.25 % w/w** and mixed thoroughly. The high-salt environment is **selective for LAB** and inhibits spoilage microorganisms.

**Step 3 — Tamping and Brine Formation (1 Mark):**
Salted cabbage is tightly packed into wooden or concrete fermentation tanks. Tamping expels residual air, and salt draws out cellular juice (osmosis) to form a natural brine that completely submerges the cabbage. The brine is covered with a lid and weighted to maintain anaerobic conditions.

**Step 4 — Microbial Succession During Fermentation (3.5 Marks):**

| **Phase** | **Duration** | **Dominant Microbes** | **Key Events** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase I — Initiation** | 0 – 24 h | Gram-negative bacteria (*Enterobacter*, *Erwinia*) | Utilise sugars, consume residual O₂ |
| **Phase II — Early Acidification** | 1 – 3 days | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* (heterofermentative) | Produces lactic acid, CO₂, ethanol, mannitol; anaerobic environment established |
| **Phase III — Main Acidification** | 3 – 14 days | *Lactobacillus plantarum, L. brevis, Pediococcus* (homofermentative dominance) | Rapid lactic acid accumulation; pH drops below 4.0; spoilage bacteria inhibited |
| **Phase IV — Final Stabilisation** | 14 – 30+ days | *L. plantarum, L. sakei* | Final sourness; flavour maturation; pH stabilises at 3.4 – 3.6 |

#### **Part (b) — Biochemistry & Quality Parameters (7 Marks)**

**Biochemistry of Fermentation (4 Marks):**

The fermentation of cabbage sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose) follows **two distinct biochemical pathways** depending on the LAB species active:

**Homofermentative Pathway (by *L. plantarum*):**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 2\,\text{ADP} + 2\,\text{P}_i \xrightarrow{\text{EMP}} 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + 2\,\text{ATP} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

Each mole of glucose yields **2 moles of lactic acid** and **2 ATP** via the Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas pathway. The enzyme **lactate dehydrogenase** catalyses the final reduction of pyruvate to lactate using NADH.

**Heterofermentative Pathway (by *Leuconostoc*):**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{ADP} + \text{P}_i \xrightarrow{\text{6-P-Gluconate}} \text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{ATP}
$$

Each mole of glucose yields **1 mole each of lactic acid, ethanol, and CO₂** with only **1 ATP** net gain. The **phosphoketolase enzyme** is the hallmark of this pathway.

**Quality Parameters of Final Product (3 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Standard Value** |
|---|---|
| **pH** | 3.4 – 3.6 |
| **Titratable Acidity (as lactic acid)** | 0.8 – 1.5 % |
| **Salt content** | 1.5 – 2.0 % |
| **Colour** | Pale yellow to light golden |
| **Texture** | Crisp, uniform shreds |
| **Flavour** | Clean lactic sourness, no off-odour |
| **Microbial load** | Total plate count < 10⁴ CFU/g after pasteurisation |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**Compare the manufacturing processes, microbial flora, and biochemical pathways involved in Kimchi and Cucumber Pickle fermentation. Discuss the role of spices in Kimchi and the role of CaCl₂ in pickle manufacture.**

#### **Part (a) — Comparative Process & Microbiology (7 Marks)**

**Kimchi Manufacturing Process (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Kimjang (Pre-salting):** Napa cabbage is quartered, washed, and brined in **10 – 15 % NaCl** for 8 – 12 hours. The high salt concentration (much higher than sauerkraut) softens cell walls and pre-selects LAB.
2. **Rinse & Drain:** Brined cabbage is rinsed 2 – 3 times to remove excess salt.
3. **Yangnyeom Application:** A seasoning paste is prepared from garlic, ginger, *gochugaru* (red pepper powder), fish sauce, glutinous rice paste, and julienned radish. The paste is massaged between cabbage leaves.
4. **Fermentation:** Packed tightly in *onggi* (earthenware) or food-grade containers and fermented at **4 – 10 °C** for 1 – 21 days.
5. **Storage:** Stored at **0 °C** in dedicated kimchi refrigerators.

**Cucumber Pickle Manufacturing Process (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Grading & Washing:** Fresh cucumbers (3 – 8 cm size) are washed, graded by size, and trimmed.
2. **Brining:** Cucumbers are packed in **5 – 8 % NaCl brine** containing **0.1 – 0.2 % CaCl₂** for texture firming.
3. **Fermentation:** Natural or starter culture fermentation at **20 – 30 °C** for 7 – 14 days. The progression: *Leuconostoc* (early) → *Lactobacillus plantarum* + *Pediococcus* (late).
4. **Post-Fermentation:** Either refrigerated as fresh-packed dills, or pasteurised at **74 °C / 15 min** for shelf-stable pickles.

**Comparative Microbiology Table:**

| **Aspect** | **Kimchi** | **Pickles** |
|---|---|---|
| **Salt Level** | 5 – 8 % (initial brine higher for pre-soak) | 5 – 10 % |
| **Temperature** | 4 – 10 °C | 20 – 30 °C |
| **Early LAB** | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides, Weissella koreensis* | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* |
| **Late LAB** | *L. plantarum, L. sakei* | *L. plantarum, P. cerevisiae, L. brevis* |
| **Final pH** | 3.8 – 4.2 | 3.5 – 5.0 |
| **Duration** | 1 – 21 days | 7 – 14 days |

#### **Part (b) — Role of Spices & CaCl₂ (7 Marks)**

**Role of Spices in Kimchi (3.5 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> Spices in kimchi serve **multifunctional** roles extending beyond flavour:

1. **Antimicrobial Activity:** Garlic, ginger, and red pepper (*gochugaru*) contain bioactive compounds (allicin, gingerols, capsaicin) that inhibit pathogenic bacteria like *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, and *Listeria*.
2. **Flavour Development:** Spice compounds (allyl sulfides, capsaicinoids) react with LAB metabolites to form unique aromatic compounds via **Maillard-like reactions** during fermentation.
3. **Antioxidant Capacity:** Red pepper and garlic provide phenolics and flavonoids that protect the product from oxidative spoilage.
4. **Texture Modification:** Fish sauce provides nitrogenous compounds that support LAB growth; glutinous rice paste supplies fermentable sugars for sustained fermentation.
5. **Health Benefits:** Spices contribute **anti-inflammatory, anti-obesity, and cardioprotective** bioactive compounds to kimchi, enhancing its **functional food** status.

**Role of CaCl₂ in Pickle Manufacture (3.5 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Calcium Chloride (CaCl₂)** is added at **0.1 – 0.2 %** during cucumber brining for the following reasons:

1. **Texture Firming:** Ca²⁺ ions crosslink with **pectin molecules** in cucumber cell walls, forming **calcium pectate (egg-box model)**, which strengthens tissue integrity and prevents softening.
2. **Prevention of Bloater Damage:** Firm cucumbers resist gas buildup by heterofermentative LAB during early fermentation, reducing the incidence of **"bloaters"** (hollow pickles).
3. **Microbial Modulation:** CaCl₂ slightly inhibits pectinolytic spoilage bacteria and fungi, extending shelf life.
4. **Brine Stability:** Maintains the structural integrity of cucumbers during prolonged brine storage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **MISTAKE 1:** Confusing **heterofermentative** end products. The correct equation is **1 lactic acid + 1 ethanol + 1 CO₂**, NOT 2 lactic acid. Marks are deducted if students write the homofermentative equation for *Leuconostoc*.
> - **MISTAKE 2:** Stating the **wrong pH range** for sauerkraut (3.4 – 3.6). Examiners specifically test for this exact range.
> - **MISTAKE 3:** Failing to mention **CaCl₂'s role in pickle texture firming** (egg-box model of pectin crosslinking). This is a guaranteed 2-mark question in some form.
> - **MISTAKE 4:** Listing *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* as the **final** dominant species. It dominates only **early fermentation**; *L. plantarum* dominates the **late** phase.
> - **MISTAKE 5:** Confusing **Kimchi's salt level (5 – 8 %)** with sauerkraut's (2 – 2.5 %). This is a frequently tested comparison.
> - **MISTAKE 6:** Not specifying the **temperature ranges** for each fermentation. Examiners look for 18 – 22 °C (sauerkraut), 4 – 10 °C (kimchi), 20 – 30 °C (pickles).
> - **MISTAKE 7:** Writing the **Homofermentative pathway equation** as $2\,C_3H_6O_3$ without specifying the **2 ATP** energy yield. Both stoichiometry and energetics must be stated.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield KTU Points:**

- ✅ **LAB Definition:** Gram-positive, non-spore forming, catalase-negative, aerotolerant anaerobes.
- ✅ **Homofermentative LAB:** EMP pathway → 2 lactic acid + 2 ATP per glucose.
- ✅ **Heterofermentative LAB:** Phosphoketolase pathway → 1 lactic acid + 1 ethanol + 1 CO₂ + 1 ATP per glucose.
- ✅ **Microbial Succession Order:** *Enterobacter/Erwinia* → *Leuconostoc* → *Lactobacillus* → *L. plantarum* (final).
- ✅ **Sauerkraut:** 2.25 % NaCl, 18 – 22 °C, pH 3.4 – 3.6, 14 – 30 days.
- ✅ **Kimchi:** 5 – 8 % NaCl, 4 – 10 °C, pH 3.8 – 4.2, 1 – 21 days, with garlic/ginger/gochugaru spices.
- ✅ **Pickles:** 5 – 8 % NaCl + 0.1 – 0.2 % CaCl₂, 20 – 30 °C, pH 3.5 – 5.0, 7 – 14 days.
- ✅ **CaCl₂ Function:** Pectin crosslinking → texture firming (egg-box model) + bloaters prevention.
- ✅ **Sauerkraut Phases:** I-Initiation, II-Early Acidification, III-Main Acidification, IV-Final Stabilisation.
- ✅ **Kimchi Storage:** 0 °C in kimchi refrigerators to prevent over-acidification.
- ✅ **Heterofermentative organisms dominate EARLY fermentation** (Phase II), while homofermentative organisms dominate LATE fermentation (Phase III-IV).
- ✅ **Salt Functions:** Selective inhibition of Gram-negative spoilage bacteria, osmotic brine formation, texture maintenance, flavour contribution.
- ✅ **Industrial Relevance:** LAB fermentation is foundational to bio-preservation, probiotic food development, and bioplastic (PLA) production.

> [!NOTE]
> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — FERMENTED FRUITS AND VEGETABLE PRODUCTS**
> **For Further Reference:** Refer to KTU prescribed textbooks on Food Fermentation Technology and standard microbiology references on Lactic Acid Bacteria. Practice numerical problems on fermentation stoichiometry and ATP yield calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermented Cereal and Legume Products: Bread, Rice-Based and Soy-Based Products - Biochemical Changes During Fermentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT528 - FOOD FERMENTATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fermented Cereal and Legume Products: Bread, Rice-Based and Soy-Based Products - Biochemical Changes During Fermentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fermented Cereal and Legume Products** are food commodities produced by the controlled microbial transformation (primarily by *Lactic Acid Bacteria* (LAB), *Yeasts*, and *Filamentous Fungi*) of substrates derived from cereals (wheat, rice, maize, sorghum, millet) and legumes (soybean, black gram, chickpea). Fermentation induces a series of **biochemical changes**—including enzymatic hydrolysis of starch and proteins, generation of organic acids and alcohols, synthesis of flavor volatiles, leavening by CO₂, and degradation of anti-nutritional factors—thereby improving the *shelf-life, nutritional profile, texture, palatability, and digestibility* of the raw substrate.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEFTT528, Module 3)**, the three cornerstone product categories covered are:

| Product Class | Representative Examples | Primary Microorganisms |
|---|---|---|
| **Bread** | Sourdough, Wheat-leavened bread | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, *Lactobacillus sanfranciscensis*, *L. plantarum* |
| **Rice-Based** | Idli, Dosa batter, Tapai, Rice Beer (Pombe) | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*, *L. plantarum*, *S. cerevisiae*, *Amylomyces rouxii* |
| **Soy-Based** | Tempeh, Miso, Natto, Soy Sauce (Shoyu) | *Rhizopus oligosporus*, *Aspergillus oryzae*, *Bacillus subtilis natto*, *Tetragenococcus halophilus* |

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Bakery's Hidden Alchemy"**
> Imagine a wheat grain as a **locked bank vault**. Inside the vault are huge energy reserves (starch) and structural proteins (gluten), but they are inaccessible to the human digestive system. **Fermentation is the master locksmith**: microorganisms release specialized enzymatic "keys" (amylases, proteases, phytases) that crack the vault open. As the locksmiths work, they:
> - **Burn some treasure** to produce CO₂ (which inflates dough like a hot air balloon) and ethanol (which evaporates, carrying aroma).
> - **Leave behind a tip** — organic acids, B-vitamins, and bioactive peptides — that fortifies the food.
> - **Deactivate the security alarms** (phytates, lectins, trypsin inhibitors) that would otherwise block mineral absorption and digestion.

The end result: a softer, tastier, more nutritious, and safer product than the raw grain.

## 1.3 Key Biochemical Reactions (Overview)

The three master reactions that govern ALL fermented cereal/legume systems are:

1. **Carbohydrate Metabolism:**
   $$\text{Starch} \xrightarrow{\text{Amylases}} \text{Maltose/Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{Glycolysis}} \text{Lactic Acid / Ethanol + CO}_2$$

2. **Protein Metabolism:**
   $$\text{Polypeptides} \xrightarrow{\text{Proteases}} \text{Peptides} \xrightarrow{\text{Peptidases}} \text{Free Amino Acids} \rightarrow \text{Aroma Compounds}$$

3. **Lipid & Anti-nutrient Metabolism:**
   $$\text{ triglycerides, phytates, lectins} \xrightarrow{\text{Lipases, Phytases}} \text{Free Fatty Acids, Inositol, Available Minerals}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Always begin a fermentation question by classifying the substrate (cereal vs. legume), naming the starter culture, and identifying the **dominant biochemical change** (acidification, leavening, proteolysis, or anti-nutrient reduction). Examiners reward this systematic approach.

---

## 1.4 Specific Product Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Bread (Sourdough & Yeast-Leavened):** A baked product made from wheat flour, water, salt, and a fermenting agent. In *sourdough*, a stable consortium of **heterofermentative LAB** (e.g., *L. sanfranciscensis*) and *S. cerevisiae* produces CO₂ for leavening and organic acids for flavor. In *industrial bread*, the primary fermenter is *S. cerevisiae* (baker's yeast).

> [!NOTE]
> **Idli/Dosa Batter:** A naturally fermented dough of *parboiled rice* and *dehusked black gram (urad dal)* in roughly 2:1 to 4:1 ratio, fermented 8–24 h at ~30 °C. The dominant microflora are *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*, *Lactobacillus plantarum*, *Streptococcus faecalis*, and yeasts. The fermentation yields a leavened, mildly acidic batter that produces soft idlis and crisp dosas upon cooking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tempeh:** An Indonesian fermented soybean cake bound into a firm slice by the dense mycelium of *Rhizopus oligosporus* (or *R. oryzae*/*R. stolonifer*). Solid-state fermentation at ~30 °C for 24–48 h produces a **textured, sliceable, mushroom-flavored, high-protein product** with markedly reduced phytic acid and trypsin inhibitors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Miso & Soy Sauce (Shoyu):** Japanese fermented soybean pastes/liquors produced by **two-stage fermentation** — first a *koji* stage with *Aspergillus oryzae* (which generates amylases, proteases, and lipases), followed by a brine fermentation with LAB (*Tetragenococcus halophilus*) and yeasts (*Zygosaccharomyces rouxii*) lasting 6–24 months.

> [!NOTE]
> **Natto:** A Japanese sticky, ammonia-flavored, viscous fermented soybean product made by fermenting cooked soybeans with *Bacillus subtilis var. natto* at 40–45 °C for 18–24 h. The characteristic stringiness comes from **poly-γ-glutamic acid (γ-PGA)** and slime from levan (a fructan polymer).

---

## 1.5 Why Fermentation? — The "Big Four" Drivers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four universal advantages conferred by fermentation of cereals/legumes are:
> 1. **Improved Digestibility & Bioavailability** — partial starch hydrolysis lowers glycemic load; phytate breakdown releases bound minerals (Ca²⁺, Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺).
> 2. **Extended Shelf-life** — drop in pH (typically to 3.5–4.5) and production of bacteriocins inhibit spoilage organisms.
> 3. **Enhanced Nutritional Value** — microbial biosynthesis of B-vitamins (riboflavin, niacin, B₆, folate) and essential amino acids (lysine, methionine).
> 4. **Improved Organoleptic Quality** — development of characteristic flavors, aromas, and textures (e.g., bread crumb, idli fluffiness, tempeh firmness).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Substrate Composition of Cereals vs. Legumes

Understanding the substrate is essential because the **direction of fermentation biochemistry is dictated by the raw material's macro-composition**.

| Component | Cereal (Wheat / Rice) | Legume (Soybean) | Fermentation Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Starch** | 60–80 % (dominant) | 1–5 % (minor) | Cereals → strong amylolytic activity |
| **Protein** | 8–15 % (gluten in wheat) | 35–45 % | Legumes → dominant proteolytic activity |
| **Lipid** | 1–3 % | 18–22 % | Legumes → lipase action, FFA generation |
| **Fiber** | 2–12 % | 5–15 % | Cellulose/hemicellulose softening |
| **Phytate** | 0.5–1.5 % | 1.0–2.5 % | Both need phytase for mineral release |
| **Anti-nutrients** | Low | High (lectins, TI, saponins, oxalates) | Legume fermentation is *mandatory* for safety |
| **Lysine** | Limiting AA | Abundant | Cereal-legume blends → complementary protein |

## 2.2 Biochemical Changes — Systematic Classification

The biochemical changes during fermentation can be grouped into **six major pathways**. Each is essential for KTU answer writing.

### 2.2.1 Pathway I — Carbohydrate Metabolism (Starch Hydrolysis & Fermentation)

**Step 1 — Starch Hydrolysis:**
$$\text{Starch (amylose + amylopectin)} \xrightarrow{\alpha\text{-amylase}} \text{Maltodextrins} \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-amylase, maltase, glucoamylase}} \text{Glucose}$$

**Step 2 — Homolactic Fermentation (Homofermentative LAB):**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{LDH} 2\,CH_3CHOHCOOH \quad (\Delta G^0 = -198\,kJ/mol)$$

**Step 3 — Heterolactic Fermentation (Heterofermentative LAB, e.g., *L. sanfranciscensis*):**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CHOHCOOH + CO_2 + C_2H_5OH \quad (\Delta G^0 = -225\,kJ/mol)$$

**Step 4 — Alcoholic Fermentation (*S. cerevisiae* in bread & rice beer):**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \quad (\Delta G^0 = -238\,kJ/mol)$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** In **sourdough**, the *L. sanfranciscensis / S. cerevisiae* co-culture exhibits **maltose metabolism synergy** — LAB uses maltose via constitutive maltose phosphorylase (consuming Pi, raising pH), while yeast secretes glucose from sucrose, lowering pH. This metabolic "push-pull" stabilizes dough pH around 4.0–4.5.

### 2.2.2 Pathway II — Protein Metabolism (Proteolysis & Aroma Generation)

**Step 1 — Proteolysis:**
$$\text{Storage proteins (gluten, glycinin, β-conglycinin)} \xrightarrow{Acid + Microbial Proteases} \text{Peptides} \xrightarrow{Peptidases} \text{Free Amino Acids (FAA)}$$

**Step 2 — Free Amino Acid Catabolism (Aroma Generation):**
The liberated FAA undergo **transamination, decarboxylation, and reduction** to yield aroma-active volatiles:

| Precursor Amino Acid | Enzyme | Volatile Product | Aroma |
|---|---|---|---|
| Leucine | α-ketoacid decarboxylase | 3-Methylbutanal | Malty |
| Phenylalanine | PAL/Decarboxylase | 2-Phenylethanol | Rose-like |
| Methionine | Demethiolase | Methional | Boiled potato |
| Valine | α-ketoacid decarboxylase | 2-Methylpropanal | Malty |
| Tyrosine | Hydroxylase | 4-Hydroxyphenylethanol | Sweet |
| Tryptophan | Decarboxylase | Tryptophol | Floral |

**Step 3 — Biogenic Amine Formation (Undesirable but testable):**
$$FAA \xrightarrow{\text{Amino acid decarboxylase}} \text{Biogenic amines (tyramine, histamine, putrescine)}$$
- High in miso and tempeh; monitored as **food safety parameter**.

### 2.2.3 Pathway III — Lipid Metabolism

$$\text{Triglycerides} \xrightarrow{\text{Lipases}} \text{Glycerol + Free Fatty Acids} \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-oxidation}} \text{Methyl Ketones, Lactones, Aldehydes}$$

> In tempeh, *Rhizopus* lipases liberate **linoleic and linolenic acids** which are then oxidized to flavor-active **carbonyls** (hexanal, 2,4-decadienal) responsible for the beany/mushroom aroma.

### 2.2.4 Pathway IV — Anti-nutritional Factor Degradation (Crucial for Legumes)

| Anti-nutrient | Effect | Fermentation-Induced Degradation |
|---|---|---|
| **Phytic acid (IP6)** | Chelates Ca²⁺, Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺ | **Phytase** → IP6 → IP5 → IP3 → IP2 → Inositol + Pi (typically 30–70 % reduction) |
| **Trypsin Inhibitors (Kunitz, Bowman-Birk)** | Block trypsin, cause pancreatic hypertrophy | Acid + heat + proteolysis → **60–95 % inactivation** in tempeh/miso |
| **Lectins (Soybean hemagglutinin)** | Agglutinate RBCs, GI toxicity | Heat denaturation + proteolysis → **>90 % reduction** in tempeh |
| **Raffinose & Stachyose** | Cause flatulence (α-galactosides) | **α-galactosidase** → Galactose + Sucrose (60–90 % reduction in idli) |
| **Saponins** | Bitter, hemolytic | β-glucosidases → partial hydrolysis |
| **Tannins, Oxalates, Urease** | Reduce protein digestibility | Acid + enzyme hydrolysis |

### 2.2.5 Pathway V — Vitamin Synthesis & Bio-fortification

Fermenting microorganisms *de novo* synthesize:
- **B-group vitamins:** Thiamine (B₁), Riboflavin (B₂), Niacin (B₃), Pyridoxine (B₆), Folate (B₉), Cobalamin (B₁₂ in natto only).
- **Vitamin K₂ (menaquinone-7)** — uniquely abundant in **natto**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **natto** is the only non-animal, plant-derived commercial source of bioavailable **vitamin B₁₂**.

### 2.2.6 Pathway VI — Exopolysaccharide (EPS) & Texture Formation

- **Dextrans** (α-1,6-glucan) — produced by *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* in idli batter; contribute to **viscosity and gas-holding capacity**.
- **Pullulan** — produced by *Aureobasidium pullulans*; food-grade biopolymer.
- **Poly-γ-glutamic acid (γ-PGA)** — produced by *Bacillus subtilis natto*; gives natto its **sticky slime**.
- **Mycelial network** of *Rhizopus* in tempeh physically binds soybean cotyledons into a **textured cake** (cell density ~10⁶–10⁷ propagules/g).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is a comprehensive table of all quantitative concepts, constants, and boundary conditions that KTU examiners test. Memorize these.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific growth rate (μ) | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$ (Monod) | 0.2–0.6 | h⁻¹ |
| 2 | Lactic acid yield (homo) | $Y_{LA} = 1.0\,g/g$ glucose | 0.95–1.0 | g·g⁻¹ |
| 3 | Lactic acid yield (hetero) | $Y_{LA} = 0.5\,g/g$ glucose | 0.45–0.55 | g·g⁻¹ |
| 4 | Ethanol yield (yeast) | $Y_{EtOH} = 0.51\,g/g$ glucose (theoretical 0.511) | 0.45–0.50 | g·g⁻¹ |
| 5 | CO₂ yield (yeast) | $Y_{CO_2} = 0.49\,g/g$ glucose (theoretical 0.489) | 0.44–0.48 | g·g⁻¹ |
| 6 | Total Titratable Acidity (TTA) | $TTA = \dfrac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 100}{V_{sample}}$ (to pH 8.2) | Idli: 0.6–1.2; Tempeh: 1.0–2.5 | % lactic acid |
| 7 | pH drop (bread) | $\Delta pH = pH_{initial} - pH_{final}$ | 5.5 → 4.2 ≈ 1.3 | units |
| 8 | Fermentation index (rice beer) | $F = \dfrac{\text{Apparent extract of wort} - \text{Real extract of beer}}{\text{Apparent extract of wort}}$ | 0.55–0.75 | dimensionless |
| 9 | Phytate hydrolysis | $H_{phytate} = \dfrac{C_{0} - C_t}{C_{0}} \times 100$ | 30–70 (bread) | % |
| 10 | Volume expansion (dough) | $\Delta V / V_0$ at proof | 100–250 (wheat) | % |
| 11 | Energy from ethanol combustion | $C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O$, $\Delta H = -1366\,kJ/mol$ | — | kJ·mol⁻¹ |
| 12 | Henderson–Hasselbalch (pKa lactic acid 3.86) | $pH = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | Used in buffer design | — |
| 13 | Moisture content (Tempeh) | $M = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | 55–65 | % w.b. |
| 14 | Water activity | $a_w$ | Bread crust: 0.65; Tempeh: 0.95 | dimensionless |
| 15 | Critical TTI reduction | $R_{TI} = \dfrac{A_{initial} - A_{final}}{A_{initial}}$ | >0.8 (legume) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **No `\|` in any cell!** All absolute-value notations use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Industrial Use |
|---|---|
| **Sourdough biotechnology** | Replacement of chemical preservatives, natural flavor booster in clean-label bakery |
| **Idli/dosa batter** | Commercially packed, refrigerated, long-shelf-life batter industries in India (MTR, iD Fresh) |
| **Tempeh** | Global plant-based protein; substitute for meat; sustainable protein source |
| **Miso & soy sauce** | Global condiment industry, multi-billion USD market |
| **Natto** | Functional food; vitamin K₂ supplement; cardiovascular health |
| **Rice beer** | Ethnic beverage (Pombe, Oji, Sonti) + ethanol fuel research |
| **Phytase-rich starters** | Biofortification of cereal flours for iron/zinc-deficient populations |
| **Enzymatic process water (koji)** | Source of industrial proteases, amylases for food & textile industry |

---

## 2.5 Product-Specific Biochemical Highlights

### 2.5.1 Bread
- **Gas retention:** CO₂ trapped by gluten network → sponge-like crumb.
- **Crust flavor:** Maillard + caramelization at 180–220 °C.
- **Crumb texture:** Starch retrogradation post-bake → staling.
- **Anti-staling role:** Sourdough LAB produce **EPS** that retard retrogradation.

### 2.5.2 Idli & Dosa
- **Natural leavening:** Mixed LAB + yeasts + gas.
- **Dosa crispness:** Acidic, dextran-rich batter produces thin, crisp crepe.
- **Idli fluffiness:** Trapped CO₂ + steam in small pores.
- **Nutritional gain:** Significant reduction of **raffinose, stachyose** (anti-flatulence).

### 2.5.3 Tempeh
- **Texture:** White mycelial mat binds cotyledons; **slicable** at pH 4.5–5.0.
- **Nutritional gain:** Protein digestibility ↑ from ~65 % to >85 %; B₁₂ trace; isoflavones converted to **aglycones** (more bioactive).

### 2.5.4 Miso / Soy Sauce
- **Two-stage:** *Koji* (mold) → *Moromi* (yeast + LAB in brine).
- **Long maturation:** 6–24 months → deep umami via glutamate release.

### 2.5.5 Natto
- **Stringiness:** γ-PGA (viscous polymer).
- **Distinctive aroma:** Pyrazines (2,5-dimethylpyrazine) from *Bacillus* metabolism.
- **Vitamin K₂:** 1000+ μg/100 g — richest plant source.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, PATHWAYS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Methodology Note:** Because fermentation technology is fundamentally a **microbial biochemistry + process engineering** discipline, the "derivations" below consist of:
> (a) Full **stoichiometric derivations** of key biochemical equations (mass & energy balances).
> (b) **Enzymatic mechanism walkthroughs**.
> (c) **Quantitative worked numerical problems** (KTU Part B style).
> No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (*S. cerevisiae*)

The **Gay-Lussac equation** (1839) is the foundation of industrial ethanol stoichiometry.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced molecular equation:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (yeast)}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Step 2 — Determine molar masses (g·mol⁻¹):**

$$M_{glucose} = 180.16 \quad M_{ethanol} = 46.07 \quad M_{CO_2} = 44.01$$

**Step 3 — Compute theoretical mass yield (Y_ethanol/sugar):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{EtOH} &= \frac{\text{Mass of ethanol produced}}{\text{Mass of glucose consumed}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 46.07}{1 \times 180.16} \\
&= \frac{92.14}{180.16} \\
&= 0.5114 \quad \left[\frac{g\,EtOH}{g\,glucose}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volume percent (industrial relevance, 1 mol ethanol = 58.4 mL at 20 °C):**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{EtOH} &= 2 \times 58.4 = 116.8 \quad [mL] \\
V_{glucose\,solution} &\approx 1000 \quad [mL] \quad \text{(for 1 M)} \\
\%\,v/v &= 11.68\,\% \text{ ethanol (theoretical)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Real-world (industrial) correction:**

$$Y_{industrial} = 0.90 \times Y_{theoretical} = 0.90 \times 0.5114 = 0.460 \quad [g/g]$$

This 90% factor accounts for biomass synthesis (≈ 5%), glycerol (≈ 4%), and minor byproducts (acetaldehyde, fusel alcohols ≈ 1%).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** "**0.51 g/g — the magic number**" for ethanol from glucose. Almost every fermentation calculation problem tests this.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Theoretical Lactic Acid Yield (Homofermentative LAB)

**Step 1 — Balanced homolactic equation:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{LDH} 2\,CH_3CHOHCOOH$$

**Step 2 — Compute mass yield:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{LA} &= \frac{2 \times 90.08}{1 \times 180.16} \\
&= \frac{180.16}{180.16} \\
&= 1.000 \quad \left[\frac{g\,LA}{g\,glucose}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Practical correction (industrially):**

$$Y_{LA,\,practical} = 0.95 \times 1.000 = 0.950 \quad [g/g]$$

(5% loss to biomass, minor organic acids, exopolysaccharides.)

> [!NOTE]
> Compare: **Yeast → 0.51 g ethanol/g glucose**, **Homo-LAB → 1.00 g lactic acid/g glucose**. This is the **single most important comparative fact** for KTU Board Exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Theoretical CO₂ Production in Bread (Volume Basis)

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric basis:** 1 mol glucose → 2 mol CO₂.

**Step 2 — Volume of CO₂ at STP per kg of glucose:**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{CO_2} &= \frac{2\,mol\,CO_2}{1\,mol\,glucose} = \frac{2 \times 1000}{180.16} = 11.10 \quad [mol] \\
V_{CO_2} \text{ at STP} &= 11.10 \times 22.4 = 248.7 \quad [L] \\
V_{CO_2} \text{ at 30 °C (proof temp)} &= 11.10 \times 24.47 = 271.6 \quad [L]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Dough expansion estimate:**
A 500 g dough (≈ 60% water, 40% flour, ≈ 20% starch hydrolyzable) yields:
$$\approx 20\,g \times 11.10 \times 24.47/1000 \approx 5.4\,L\,CO_2$$
→ Inflates dough by 100–250%.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Phytate Hydrolysis Kinetics in Tempeh

Phytic acid hydrolysis follows pseudo-first-order kinetics:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k_{phytase} \cdot C$$

**Integrating** with $C(0) = C_0$:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k\,t}$$

**Fraction hydrolyzed:**

$$H(t) = 1 - e^{-k\,t}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical (KTU Board Practice):**

> **Problem:** In tempeh fermentation at 30 °C, initial phytic acid = **15 mg/g** dry soybean. After 36 h, residual phytic acid = **4.5 mg/g**. Calculate **(a)** the first-order rate constant *k*, **(b)** the time to reach 90 % hydrolysis.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 15 \quad [mg/g] \\
C(36) &= 4.5 \quad [mg/g] \\
4.5 &= 15 \cdot e^{-k \cdot 36} \\
e^{-36k} &= \frac{4.5}{15} = 0.30 \\
-36k &= \ln(0.30) = -1.204 \\
k &= \frac{1.204}{36} = 0.0334 \quad [h^{-1}]
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= 0.90 \Rightarrow C = 0.10\,C_0 \\
0.10\,C_0 &= C_0 \cdot e^{-k\,t} \\
-0.0334\,t &= \ln(0.10) = -2.303 \\
t_{90} &= \frac{2.303}{0.0334} = 68.95 \quad [h] \approx 69\,h
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** ~90 % phytate reduction requires ~69 h, consistent with industrial tempeh extended fermentations.

---

## 3.5 Derivation V — Energy Yield in LAB vs. Yeast

For an engineer, comparing **ATP yield per mole of glucose** explains why LAB are "inefficient" but LAB products dominate cereal/legume fermentations.

| Pathway | Net ATP/mol glucose | ΔG° (kJ/mol) | Microbe |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EMP + Homolactic** | 2 ATP | −198 | *L. plantarum* |
| **6-P-Gluconate / Phosphoketolase** | 1 ATP | −225 | *L. sanfranciscensis* |
| **EMP + Alcoholic** | 2 ATP | −238 | *S. cerevisiae* |
| **TCA cycle (aerobic)** | 38 ATP | −2870 | *Aspergillus oryzae* (limited in soy) |

**Quantitative derivation (homolactic, net ATP):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{HK, PGI, PFK, ALDO, TPI, GAPDH, PGK, PGM, ENO, PK} 2\,\text{Pyruvate} \\
\text{Per GAP} \rightarrow 1\,ATP + 1\,NADH \\
2\,NADH \xrightarrow{LDH} 2\,\text{Lactate} \quad (\text{no oxidative phosphorylation, NADH reoxidized}) \\
\Rightarrow \boxed{\text{Net yield} = 2\,\text{ATP/glucose}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For yeast: NADH is reoxidized in alcoholic fermentation, so net ATP is also 2. The energy difference (ΔΔG° = −40 kJ/mol) is dissipated as heat, which is why fermentations are **exothermic** and require temperature control.

## 3.7 Quantitative Worked Example (Full KTU Part B Style)

> **Worked Problem — Idli Batter Fermentation**

> **Given:** A 1 kg idli batter is prepared with 700 g parboiled rice + 300 g black gram (urad dal) + 600 mL water + 15 g salt. Initial starch content ≈ 65 % of dry matter; initial pH = 6.2; initial TTA = 0.1 %. After 18 h fermentation at 30 °C, pH drops to 4.3 and TTA rises to 0.95 % (as lactic acid). Assuming all acid is lactic acid (MW = 90.08), calculate:
> **(a)** Total lactic acid produced (g)
> **(b)** Grams of glucose consumed (theoretical)
> **(c)** CO₂ produced (g) from heterofermentative contribution
> **(d)** % reduction in raffinose family oligosaccharides if initial RFOs = 1.2 g and final = 0.3 g

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Total lactic acid:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sample basis: TTA} &= 0.95\,\%\,w/w \text{ lactic acid} \\
\text{Batter mass} &= 1000\,g \quad (\text{approximate, ignoring salt/water loss}) \\
\text{Lactic acid produced} &= 0.0095 \times 1000 = 9.5 \quad [g]
\end{aligned}$$

> Strictly, water is 600 mL, so total mass ≈ 1600 g. Re-calculating:
> $$M_{lactic} = 0.0095 \times 1600 = 15.2 \quad [g]$$
> We'll use **15.2 g** for consistency.

**Part (b) — Glucose consumed:**

For homofermentative LAB, $Y_{LA} = 1.00\,g/g$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose} &= \frac{15.2}{1.00} = 15.2 \quad [g]
\end{aligned}$$

For heterofermentative ($Y_{LA} = 0.5\,g/g$):

$$\text{Glucose} = \frac{15.2}{0.5} = 30.4 \quad [g]$$

> Realistic answer: assume **70 % homo + 30 % hetero** mixed population:
> $$G = 0.7(15.2) + 0.3(30.4) = 10.64 + 9.12 = 19.76 \approx 19.8 \quad [g]$$

**Part (c) — CO₂ from heterofermentative fraction:**

Heterofermentative equation: $1\,C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 1\,LA + 1\,CO_2 + 1\,EtOH$

$$n_{hetero\,glucose} = \frac{9.12}{180.16} = 0.0506 \quad [mol]$$

$$n_{CO_2} = 0.0506 \quad [mol] \quad m_{CO_2} = 0.0506 \times 44.01 = 2.23 \quad [g]$$

**Part (d) — RFO reduction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{RFO} &= \frac{1.2 - 0.3}{1.2} \times 100 \\
&= 75\,\% \text{ reduction}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Final answers:** (a) **15.2 g** lactic acid, (b) **~19.8 g** glucose consumed, (c) **~2.23 g** CO₂, (d) **75 %** RFO reduction.

---

## 3.8 Quantitative pH-Buffering Analysis (Henderson–Hasselbalch Application)

For a **lactic acid/lactate buffer** at pH 4.0 (typical of sourdough):

$$pH = pK_a + \log\frac{[\text{Lactate}^-]}{[\text{Lactic acid}]}$$

$$4.0 = 3.86 + \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

$$\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} = 10^{0.14} = 1.38$$

So in sourdough, the ratio of dissociated to undissociated lactic acid is ~58:42 → explains the **sharp acidic flavor**.

---

## 3.9 Summary of Key Equations for the Module

$$\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Homolactic:} \quad C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,CH_3CHOHCOOH \quad (Y = 1.00\,g/g) \\
&\textbf{Heterolactic:} \quad C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CHOHCOOH + CO_2 + C_2H_5OH \quad (Y = 0.50\,g/g) \\
&\textbf{Alcoholic:} \quad C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \quad (Y_{EtOH} = 0.511\,g/g) \\
&\textbf{Respiration:} \quad C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2 \rightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O \quad (\Delta G = -2870\,kJ/mol) \\
&\textbf{Phytate kinetics:} \quad C(t) = C_0\,e^{-k\,t} \\
&\textbf{TTA:} \quad TTA\,(\%\,LA) = \frac{V_{NaOH} \cdot N_{NaOH} \cdot 0.090}{V_{sample}} \times 100 \\
&\textbf{pH-Buffer:} \quad pH = pK_a + \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}
\end{aligned}
}$$
