## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
*Define "biogenic amines". Name four biogenic amines commonly encountered in fermented foods and the amino acid from which each is derived.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Biogenic amines (BA) are low-molecular-weight nitrogenous compounds formed by the **enzymatic decarboxylation of amino acids** by microbial amino-acid decarboxylases. (1 mark)  
Four common BA in fermented foods: **Histamine** (from Histidine), **Tyramine** (from Tyrosine), **Putrescine** (from Ornithine), and **Cadaverine** (from Lysine). (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks)  
*(Award full 3 marks if all four pairs are correctly stated.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the "Big-8" food allergens as per FSSAI/USFDA. Why is mandatory labelling required even for fermented products?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Big-8 allergens are: (1) Milk, (2) Eggs, (3) Fish, (4) Crustaceans, (5) Peanuts, (6) Tree nuts, (7) Soybeans, (8) Wheat. (1.5 marks – 0.5 × 8, but consolidated as a list; full credit if all eight appear.)  
Additionally, FSSAI includes **sesame and sulphites** > 10 mg/kg.  
**Why mandatory labelling?** Fermentation may *reduce* but does not *guarantee elimination* of allergenic epitopes; consumers with hypersensitivity depend on accurate disclosure to prevent anaphylaxis, as mandated by the **Food Safety and Standards (Labelling & Display) Regulations, 2020**. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B – 14-Mark Essay Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** *Answer any **one** full question from Module 4. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Attempt both sub-parts.*

---

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) – Choose this question

**Q A(a).** *Classify the major pathogenic microorganisms associated with fermented foods. Discuss their growth requirements, pH tolerance, and the critical control points used in their management.* **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Classification (2 marks):** Pathogens are categorised into  
   (i) **Gram-positive** – *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Staphylococcus aureus*, *Clostridium botulinum*, *Bacillus cereus*.  
   (ii) **Gram-negative** – *Salmonella* spp., *Escherichia coli* O157:H7, *Cronobacter* spp.  
   (iii) **Spore-formers** – *C. botulinum*, *B. cereus* (high heat resistance).  
   (iv) **Acid-tolerant non-sporing** – *L. monocytogenes*, *E. coli* O157:H7.

2. **Growth Requirements and pH Tolerance (3 marks):**  
   The table below summarises the key tolerance limits – mention any **four** pathogens in exam (1 mark per pathogen):

   | Pathogen | Minimum pH | Minimum a_w | Optimal T (°C) | Heat Resistance |
   |---|---|---|---|---|
   | *Salmonella* | 4.0 | 0.94 | 35–37 | D$_{70} \approx$ 0.3 min |
   | *L. monocytogenes* | 4.4 | 0.92 | 30–37 | D$_{70} \approx$ 0.4 min |
   | *C. botulinum* | 4.6 | 0.94 | 35 | D$_{121} \approx$ 0.1 min |
   | *B. cereus* | 4.4 | 0.93 | 30 | D$_{95} \approx$ 1 min |
   | *S. aureus* | 4.5 | 0.86 | 37 | D$_{70} \approx$ 0.5 min |

3. **Critical Control Points (CCP) – 2 marks:**  
   - **CCP-1: Raw material receipt** – microbiological testing (TPC, coliform).  
   - **CCP-2: Acidification** – pH $\le$ **4.6** within 48 h (controls *C. botulinum*).  
   - **CCP-3: Pasteurisation** – 72 °C / 15 s for milk (controls *E. coli*, *Salmonella*).  
   - **CCP-4: Cold-chain distribution** – $T \le$ **4 °C** for psychrotrophs (*Listeria*).

---

**Q A(b).** *Explain the principle, structure, and seven steps of the HACCP system. How is HACCP applied to a fermented dairy product (e.g., yogurt)?* **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**HACCP Definition (1 mark):** Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point – a **systematic, science-based preventive** risk-management system mandated by the **Codex Alimentarius (1993, revised 2020)** and adopted by FSSAI under Schedule 4 of the Food Safety and Standards (Licensing & Registration of Food Businesses) Regulations, 2011.

**The Seven Principles (3 marks – 0.5 each):**
1. Conduct hazard analysis.
2. Determine CCPs.
3. Establish critical limits.
4. Establish monitoring procedures.
5. Establish corrective actions.
6. Establish verification procedures.
7. Establish record-keeping and documentation.

**Application to Yogurt (3 marks):**

| CCP | Hazard | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw-milk reception | Pathogens (*Salmonella*, *Listeria*) | TPC $\le$ 10$^5$ CFU/mL; absence in 25 mL | Plate count, PCR | Reject or repasteurise |
| Pasteurisation | Pathogens | 72 °C / 15 s | Thermograph | Re-pasteurise |
| Fermentation (pH) | *C. botulinum*, *B. cereus* | pH $\le$ 4.5 in 6 h | pH probe | Extend incubation |
| Cold storage | Psychrotrophs | $\le$ 4 °C | Data logger | Recall if breached |
| Label check | Allergens (milk) | Allergen declaration | Visual + verification | Re-label or recall |

---

#### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**Q B(a).** *Discuss the formation of biogenic amines in fermented foods. Explain their toxicity, the legal limits, and the analytical methods used for their detection.* **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Mechanism of Formation (2 marks):** Decarboxylation of free amino acids by **microbial amino-acid decarboxylases** (PLP-dependent enzymes). Histamine, tyramine, putrescine, cadaverine are the most common. Decarboxylation is favoured at slightly acidic pH (4.5–5.5), making fermented foods an ideal matrix.

2. **Toxicological Profile (2 marks):**  
   - **Histamine** – *scombroid poisoning*: flushing, headache, urticaria, bronchospasm. Toxic dose $\approx$ 100 mg/person.  
   - **Tyramine** – *hypertensive crisis* in patients on MAO inhibitors ("cheese effect").  
   - **Putrescine / Cadaverine** – *potentiate* histamine toxicity (synergistic effect).

3. **Legal Limits (1 mark):**  
   - FDA: histamine $\le$ **50 mg/kg** in fresh fish; **100 mg/kg** in fermented products.  
   - EU Reg. 2073/2005: histamine $\le$ **100 mg/kg** (9-sample, n=9, c=2, m=100, M=200).  
   - FSSAI: aligns with Codex (absence in 25 g for pathogens; specific limits for histamine in fish products).

4. **Analytical Methods (2 marks):**  
   - **HPLC with fluorescence detection (HPLC-FLD)** after dansylation or OPA derivatisation – LOD $\sim$ **0.5 mg/kg**.  
   - **LC-MS/MS** – confirmatory, multi-analyte, LOD $< 0.1$ mg/kg.  
   - **Enzymatic kits / ELISA** – rapid screening.  
   - **TLC** – qualitative only; obsolete for regulatory work.

---

**Q B(b).** *What are food allergens? Describe the major allergens in fermented foods, the immunological basis of allergenicity, and the labelling requirements under FSSAI 2020.* **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Definition of Food Allergens (1 mark):** A *food allergen* is a normally harmless dietary protein (or hapten-protein complex) that triggers an **abnormal immune response** (Type I hypersensitivity, IgE-mediated) in sensitised individuals.

2. **Immunological Basis (2 marks):**  
   - **Sensitisation phase:** Allergen $\to$ processed by APC $\to$ presented on MHC-II to **Th2 cells** $\to$ release **IL-4, IL-5, IL-13** $\to$ class-switching to **IgE** in B-cells.  
   - **IgE binds FcεRI receptors** on mast cells and basophils.  
   - **Effector phase:** Re-exposure $\to$ cross-linking of IgE $\to$ mast-cell degranulation $\to$ release of **histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, prostaglandins** $\to$ symptoms ranging from urticaria to **anaphylaxis**.

3. **Major Allergens in Fermented Foods (2 marks):**

   | Allergen | Fermented Vehicle Example | Key Epitope |
   |---|---|---|
   | Milk (casein, $\beta$-lactoglobulin) | Cheese, yogurt, kefir | $\beta$-LG (trimeric protein) |
   | Soy (Gly m 5, Gly m 6) | Tempeh, miso, soy sauce | 7S & 11S globulins |
   | Wheat (gliadin, glutenin) | Sourdough, beer, soy sauce | Gliadin peptides |
   | Fish / shellfish | Fish sauce, shrimp paste | Tropomyosin |
   | Egg | Mayonnaise-style ferments | Ovomucoid |

   > Fermentation **reduces** but does **not eliminate** IgE-binding epitopes.

4. **Labelling Requirements under FSSAI 2020 (2 marks):**  
   - **Food Safety and Standards (Labelling & Display) Regulations, 2020**, Section 5 & Schedule II.  
   - Allergens must be declared using a **distinct font, style, or colour** in the ingredients list.  
   - **"Contains …"** statement mandatory when allergens are present.  
   - **Precautionary Allergen Labelling (PAL)** – "May contain …" for unintended cross-contamination.  
   - **"Big-8 plus sesame and sulphites > 10 mg/kg"** must be declared.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing *histamine* (a biogenic amine) with *histidine* (its parent amino acid).** Always write the *acid → amine* arrow.  
> 2. **Writing "HACCP has 7 principles" without naming them.** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the seven are not enumerated.  
> 3. **Claiming that fermentation "kills all pathogens".** It does not; *Listeria monocytogenes* and *B. cereus* spores can survive. State the survivors explicitly.  
> 4. **Mixing up *pH* and *a_w* values for *C. botulinum*.** The canonical limits are **pH 4.6** *and* **a_w 0.94** – *and*, not *or*.  
> 5. **Forgetting to mention labelling in allergen answers.** FSSAI 2020 carries 1–2 marks by itself.  
> 6. **Using non-standard units** (ppm instead of mg/kg for toxins). Stick to **mg/kg or µg/kg** as per Codex.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your 5-minute last-day revision sheet.**

- **Three Safety Pillars:** Pathogens (biological) + Toxins (chemical) + Allergens (immunological).
- **Critical pH boundary:** 4.6 (below this, *C. botulinum* cannot germinate).
- **Critical a_w boundary:** 0.85 (below this, *S. aureus* toxinogenesis halts).
- **Big-8 Allergens:** Milk, Eggs, Fish, Crustaceans, Peanuts, Tree nuts, Soy, Wheat (+ Sesame, Sulphites per FSSAI).
- **Five Major Biogenic Amines:** Histamine, Tyramine, Putrescine, Cadaverine, Tryptamine.
- **HACCP** = **7 Principles** (Codex Alimentarius, 1993/2020). Always *list* them.
- **Most heat-resistant:** *C. botulinum* spores (D$_{121} \approx$ 0.1 min but z-value high).
- **Most potent natural toxin:** Botulinum neurotoxin (LD$_{50} \approx$ 1 µg).
- **Aflatoxin B₁** is the most carcinogenic; **FSSAI limit = 20 µg/kg**.
- **Hurdle Technology (Leistner, 1992)** – synergism of pH + a_w + bacteriocins + competitive flora.
- **Michaelis–Menten kinetics** govern biogenic-amine formation; first-order at low [S].
- **Quantitative Microbial Risk Assessment (QMRA)** = Hazard × Dose × Exposure.
- **FSSAI 2020 Labelling Regulation** mandates distinct allergen declaration.
- **Two-letter rule for pH and a_w:** "pH **4.6** / a_w **0.94**" – memorise as a *pair*.
- **D-value, Z-value, F-value** – always state units (min, °C) in thermal calculations.
- **Always end a 14-mark answer** with a *practical industrial application* line – examiners reward "real-world awareness" with 0.5–1 bonus mark.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future Prospects of Food Fermentation and Advances in Fermentation Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT528 - FOOD FERMENTATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Future Prospects of Food Fermentation and Advances in Fermentation Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Future Prospects of Food Fermentation** refers to the emerging trajectory of biotechnological innovations, sustainable production paradigms, and novel product development strategies that are poised to redefine the role of microbial fermentation in the global food ecosystem through 2030 and beyond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advances in Fermentation Technology** encompass the integration of synthetic biology, systems metabolic engineering, artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), and high-throughput automation into classical fermentation science to achieve precision-controlled, scalable, and sustainable bioprocesses.

### 1.1.1 Scope of the Subject (KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping)

The topic broadly covers the following five interlinked sub-domains, which align with **Course Outcome CO4** (Apply modern biotechnological tools to enhance food fermentation processes) and **CO5** (Evaluate emerging trends and sustainability aspects in food fermentation):

1. **Novel and Unconventional Fermentation Substrates** — upcycling of agri-food waste, single-cell proteins (SCP), gas fermentation.
2. **Precision Fermentation (PF)** — microbial cell factories programmed via synthetic biology to produce specific functional molecules (proteins, enzymes, flavors, fats).
3. **Functional and Personalized Fermented Foods** — next-generation probiotics (psychobiotics, next-gen synbiotics), postbiotics, and paraprobiotics.
4. **Digitalization and Industry 4.0 in Fermentation** — AI/ML-driven bioprocess control, soft sensors, digital twins, PAT (Process Analytical Technology) tools.
5. **Sustainability, Circular Bioeconomy, and Regulatory Evolution** — carbon-neutral fermenters, cell-cultivated foods, regulatory frameworks (FDA, EFSA, FSSAI).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: "The Fermenter as a Smart Kitchen of the Future"**
>
> Imagine a traditional Indian *dosa* batter fermentation where grandma sets the batter in a brass pot overnight — relying on ambient microbes, intuition, and climatic conditions. Now imagine a **"Smart Kitchen 2050"**:
> - The brass pot is replaced by a **stainless-steel bioreactor** (precision vessel).
> - The ambient *Lactobacillus* culture is replaced by a **genetically programmed yeast** designed to produce dairy protein without a cow.
> - The window-shutter thermometer is replaced by **in-line Raman spectroscopy + AI** that adjusts temperature, pH, and feed rates in real time.
> - The leftover rice-water waste is fed **back** as substrate (circular economy).
>
> This is the **leap from traditional fermentation to advanced fermentation technology** — same biological principle, but engineered, digitized, and sustainable.

---

## 1.3 Key Terms and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Term | Standard Definition |
|------|---------------------|
| **Precision Fermentation (PF)** | Use of engineered microorganisms as **cell factories** to produce specific target molecules with high purity. |
| **Postbiotics** | Non-viable microbial cells, their metabolites, or cell-wall components that confer health benefits (**dose: typically 10⁸–10¹⁰ CFU equivalents/day**). |
| **Psychobiotics** | Probiotics that produce or stimulate neurotransmitter production (**GABA, serotonin**) impacting the gut-brain axis. |
| **Single Cell Protein (SCP)** | Biomass of microbes (bacteria, yeast, fungi, microalgae) used as protein source (**crude protein: 40–85 % dry weight**). |
| **Titer (g/L)** | Concentration of target product per litre of fermentation broth. |
| **Yield (Y_P/S, g/g)** | Grams of product per gram of substrate consumed. |
| **Productivity (g/L/h)** | Volumetric production rate, a **critical scale-up metric**. |

---

## 1.4 Real-World Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The global **fermented food market** was valued at **USD 586 billion in 2023** and is projected to surpass **USD 1 trillion by 2034 (CAGR ≈ 6.2 %)**. The **precision fermentation segment alone** is forecast to grow from **USD 4.5 billion (2023) to USD 36.3 billion by 2030 (CAGR ≈ 34.5 %)**. This represents one of the most aggressive growth profiles in the entire food-tech sector.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Precision Fermentation (PF)

### 2.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

Precision fermentation applies the tools of **synthetic biology, systems metabolic engineering, and protein engineering** to reprogram a microbial chassis (yeast, *E. coli*, filamentous fungi, lactic acid bacteria) to overproduce a **single high-value target molecule** such as:
- **Whey protein (β-lactoglobulin)** → produced by *Pichia pastoris* → marketed by Perfect Day.
- **Casein proteins** → produced by *Trichoderma reesei* → commercialized for vegan cheese.
- **Heme protein (leghemoglobin)** → produced by *Pichia pastoris* → Impossible Burger's "heme".
- **Egg white proteins (ovalbumin, lysozyme)** → produced by yeast → used in vegan baking.
- **Hyaluronic acid, collagen, vitamins (B12, D2)** → produced microbially.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Precision Fermentation

1. **Identify target molecule** — typically a complex animal-origin protein that is difficult/unsustainable to produce conventionally.
2. **Select microbial chassis** — depends on GRAS status, post-translational modification capability, and growth rate.
3. **Clone the gene** encoding the target protein into a suitable expression vector under an inducible promoter (e.g., *AOX1* for methanol-inducible expression in *Pichia*).
4. **Transform the chassis** — electroporation, lithium acetate method, or *Agrobacterium*-mediated transformation (for fungi).
5. **Optimize codon usage** and **gene copy number** for maximum expression.
6. **Scale-up** in fed-batch, perfusion, or continuous bioreactors with **in-process monitoring** (pH, DO, OD, glucose, by-products via HPLC/NIR/Raman).
7. **Downstream processing (DSP)** — cell disruption, clarification, chromatography, lyophilization.
8. **Food-grade validation** — GRAS, EFSA QPS, FSSAI approval, allergenicity and toxicity studies.

### 2.1.3 Microbial Chassis Selection Matrix

| Chassis | Strengths | Limitations | Typical Application |
|---------|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | GRAS, eukaryotic PTMs | Limited high-density growth | Egg proteins, dairy proteins |
| *Pichia pastoris* (Komagataella phaffii) | High-density growth, strong *AOX1* promoter, low secreted protein background | Methanol handling safety | Whey, casein, heme |
| *E. coli* BL21(DE3) | Fast growth, well-characterized, cheap | Endotoxins, no glycosylation | Chymosin, growth factors |
| *Corynebacterium glutamicum* | Secretes efficiently, GRAS | Slow growth | Amino acids, nucleotides |
| *Aspergillus oryzae* | Strong secretory capacity, GRAS | Filamentous — high viscosity | Enzymes, traditional koji products |
| *Lactococcus lactis* | Lactic acid bacteria — food-native, GRAS | Limited to small proteins | Antimicrobial peptides, vitamins |

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Next-Generation Probiotics and Gut-Brain Axis

### 2.2.1 The Conceptual Shift

Classical probiotics (FAO/WHO 2002 definition): **"Live microorganisms which when administered in adequate amounts confer a health benefit on the host."**

Next-generation probiotics expand this definition into:
- **Psychobiotics** — *Bifidobacterium longum* NCC3001, *Lactobacillus rhamnosus* JB-1 → reduce cortisol, anxiety.
- **Postbiotics** — inactivated cells, SCFAs, bacteriocins, cell-wall peptidoglycans.
- **Paraprobiotics** — heat-killed cells (e.g., *L. plantarum* LRCC5193).
- **Akkermansia muciniphila** — next-gen probiotic, mucin degradation, metabolic benefits.
- **Faecalibacterium prausnitzii** — butyrate producer, anti-inflammatory.

### 2.2.2 The Microbiota–Gut–Brain Axis (MGBA)

> [!NOTE]
> **MGBA** is the bidirectional communication network linking the central nervous system (CNS), the enteric nervous system (ENS), the autonomic nervous system (ANS), and the gut microbiota through **neural, endocrine, immune, and metabolic pathways**.

**Key signaling molecules:**
- **Vagus nerve** (10⁸ afferent fibers, the longest cranial nerve) — primary neural highway.
- **Short-Chain Fatty Acids (SCFAs)** — acetate, propionate, butyrate (C2, C3, C4 carboxylic acids).
- **Neurotransmitters** — GABA (γ-aminobutyric acid), serotonin (5-HT, ~90 % gut-derived), dopamine.
- **Tryptophan metabolites** — kynurenine, indole derivatives.
- **Cytokines** — IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α.

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Single-Cell Protein (SCP) and Alternative Proteins

### 2.3.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Cell Protein (SCP)** refers to the **dried biomass of pure or mixed cultures of microorganisms** (bacteria, yeasts, fungi, microalgae) used as a protein source for human food or animal feed.

### 2.3.2 Comparative Composition of SCP Sources

| Source | Crude Protein (% DW) | Lysine Content | Key Limitation |
|--------|----------------------|----------------|----------------|
| *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | 45–55 | High | High nucleic acid content (6–12 %) |
| *Candida utilis* | 50–55 | High | Nucleic acid |
| *Spirulina* (cyanobacterium) | 60–70 | Moderate | Heavy metal bioaccumulation risk |
| *Chlorella* (microalga) | 50–65 | Moderate | Cell wall digestibility |
| *Fusarium venenatum* (mycoprotein/Quorn) | 45–55 | High | Color/flavor masking required |
| *Methylococcus capsulatus* (MCP, KnipBio) | 70–85 | Very high | Methanotroph — gas substrate |

### 2.3.3 Substrates for SCP — Circular Economy Approach

- **C1 substrates**: methane, methanol, CO₂ (gas fermentation by acetogens like *Clostridium autoethanogenum*).
- **C5/C6 sugars**: from lignocellulosic hydrolysates, sugarcane bagasse, rice straw.
- **Industrial waste**: cheese whey (lactose), molasses, fruit peels, brewery spent grain.
- **C3 substrates**: glycerol from biodiesel industry.

---

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Industrial Process Analytical Technology (PAT) and AI/ML in Fermentation

### 2.4.1 The PAT Framework (FDA 2004 Guidance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Analytical Technology (PAT)** is defined by the **US FDA (2004)** as *"a system for designing, analyzing, and controlling manufacturing through timely measurements (i.e., during processing) of critical quality and performance attributes of raw and in-process materials and processes, with the goal of ensuring final product quality."*

### 2.4.2 Key PAT Tools in Advanced Fermentation

| Tool | Working Principle | Variables Measured |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **NIR Spectroscopy** (700–2500 nm) | Overtones and combinations of O–H, C–H, N–H vibrations | Glucose, ethanol, biomass, moisture |
| **Raman Spectroscopy** (785–1064 nm) | Inelastic light scattering | Multiple analytes simultaneously |
| **Fluorometry** (2D fluorescence) | Intrinsic fluorophores (NADH, tryptophan, riboflavin) | Biomass, metabolic state |
| **In-line Mass Spectrometry (MS)** | Quadrupole/time-of-flight | Exhaust gas O₂, CO₂, VOC profile |
| **Capacitance / Impedance** | Dielectric properties of cells | Viable biomass, cell size |
| **Soft Sensors** | Kalman-filter-based, AI/ML models | Hard-to-measure variables (product, by-products) |

### 2.4.3 AI/ML Bioprocess Control Loop

**Architecture:**
1. **Multimodal sensors** (pH, DO, temperature, OD, NIR, Raman) → real-time data stream at **1–10 Hz**.
2. **Edge computing / OPC-UA gateway** → data aggregation.
3. **AI/ML model** (Random Forest, LSTM, Physics-Informed Neural Networks — PINNs) → predicts product titer, glucose, lactate.
4. **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** → adjusts feed rate, agitation, aeration.
5. **Feedback loop closure** — QbD (Quality by Design) compliant.

---

## 2.5 Pillar 5 — Sustainability and Circular Bioeconomy

### 2.5.1 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)

- **Carbon footprint (kg CO₂e/kg product)** — fermentation has 60–80 % lower footprint vs. animal agriculture.
- **Water footprint (L/kg product)** — fermentation: **500–2,000 L/kg** vs. beef: **15,400 L/kg**.
- **Land use (m²·year/kg)** — fermentation requires 95–99 % less land.
- **Energy Return on Investment (EROI)**.

### 2.5.2 The 5R Hierarchy Applied to Fermentation

> [!TIP]
> **5R Hierarchy in Fermentation Sustainability:**
> 1. **Reduce** — minimize substrate, water, energy inputs.
> 2. **Reuse** — spent media, heat, biomass recycling.
> 3. **Recycle** — co-products as feedstock for other industries.
> 4. **Recover** — biogas from waste via anaerobic digestion (CH₄ yield ≈ 0.25 m³/kg VS).
> 5. **Redesign** — adopt cell-free systems, gas fermentation, electrobioreactors.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas are presented in a markdown table. Note: All absolute value / division operators are escaped (`\vert` / `\mid`) to prevent markdown breakage.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Volumetric Productivity | $P_v = \dfrac{C_{P,f} - C_{P,0}}{t_f - t_0}$ | $C_P$ = product concentration (g/L), $t$ = time (h) | Compare batch vs. fed-batch |
| 2 | Biomass Yield on Substrate | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | $\Delta X$ = biomass produced (g), $\Delta S$ = substrate consumed (g) | Substrate efficiency |
| 3 | Product Yield on Substrate | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | $\Delta P$ = product (g), $\Delta S$ = substrate (g) | Carbon balance |
| 4 | Specific Growth Rate | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ (Monod) | $X$ = biomass (g/L), $S$ = substrate (g/L), $K_s$ = half-saturation const. (g/L) | Microbial kinetics |
| 5 | Doubling Time | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹) | Strain comparison |
| 6 | Mass Balance (Stoichiometric) | $a\,C_6H_{12}O_6 + b\,NH_3 \rightarrow c\,CH_{1.8}O_{0.5}N_{0.2} + d\,CO_2 + e\,H_2O$ | $a, b, c, d, e$ = stoichiometric coefficients | Elemental balance |
| 7 | Oxygen Transfer Rate | $OTR = k_La \cdot (C^* - C_L)$ | $k_La$ = volumetric mass transfer coef. (h⁻¹), $C^*$ = saturation DO (mg/L), $C_L$ = actual DO (mg/L) | Aerobic scale-up |
| 8 | OUR / CPR relation | $OUR = CPR \cdot RQ^{-1}$ | $OUR$ = O₂ uptake rate, $CPR$ = CO₂ production rate, $RQ$ = respiratory quotient | Gas analysis |
| 9 | Henderson–Hasselbalch (pH control) | $pH = pK_a + \log_{10} \dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | $pK_a$ = acid dissociation const. | Buffer design |
| 10 | Logistic Growth Model | $X(t) = \dfrac{X_{max}}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_m)}}$ | $X_{max}$ = max biomass, $k$ = rate const., $t_m$ = inflection time | Fermentation modeling |
| 11 | Gompertz (modified) | $P = P_{max} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot e}{P_{max}}(\lambda - t) + 1\right)\right)$ | $P_{max}$ = max product, $\lambda$ = lag time | Curve fitting |
| 12 | Scale-up (Constant P/V) | $\left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)_1 = \left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)_2$ | $P$ = power input (W), $V$ = volume (m³) | Geometric similarity |
| 13 | Scale-up (Constant $k_La$) | $k_La \propto \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.56} \cdot v_s^{0.31}$ | $P_g$ = gassed power, $v_s$ = superficial gas velocity | Aerobic scale-up |
| 14 | Specific Death Rate (Thermal) | $k_d = \dfrac{1}{t} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | $N_0$ = initial count, $N$ = survivors | Pasteurization design |
| 15 | Energy Balance (Bioreactor) | $Q_{acc} = Q_{gen} - Q_{rem} - Q_{loss}$ | $Q$ = heat flow (W) | Heat removal design |
| 16 | RQ (Aerobic) | $RQ = \dfrac{CO_2 \text{ produced}}{O_2 \text{ consumed}}$ | Moles (or volumes) of gases | Metabolic flux |
| 17 | Mass Yield (Gas Ferm.) | $Y_{X/CO_2} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{n_{CO_2}}$ | $X$ = biomass (g), $n_{CO_2}$ = moles CO₂ fixed | Acetogen efficiency |
| 18 | Net Protein Utilization (NPU) | $NPU = \dfrac{N_{retained}}{N_{intake}} \times 100$ | $N$ = nitrogen (g) | Nutritional quality |
| 19 | PER | $PER = \dfrac{\text{weight gain (g)}}{\text{protein intake (g)}}$ | Dimensionless | Protein quality |
| 20 | Thermodynamic Efficiency (cell) | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{\Delta G_{biomass}}{\Delta G_{substrate}}$ | $\Delta G$ = Gibbs free energy (kJ/mol) | Theoretical max yield |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Monod Kinetics and Maximum Dilution Rate in Continuous Culture

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Derive the **critical dilution rate ($D_{crit}$)** at which washout occurs in a **chemostat (CSTR)** operating with a microorganism following Monod kinetics, given:
- Maximum specific growth rate $\mu_{max} = 0.5\ \text{h}^{-1}$
- Half-saturation constant $K_s = 0.2\ \text{g/L}$
- Inlet substrate concentration $S_{in} = 20\ \text{g/L}$

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Start with the Monod equation.**

$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$$

> *Logic:* The Monod equation empirically describes how specific growth rate $\mu$ depends on the limiting substrate concentration $S$. When $S \gg K_s$, $\mu \to \mu_{max}$; when $S \ll K_s$, $\mu \approx (\mu_{max}/K_s) \cdot S$ (first-order).

**Step 2: Write the steady-state mass balance for biomass in a chemostat.**

$$\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu X - D X = 0$$

> *Logic:* In a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) at steady state, the rate of biomass production ($\mu X$) equals the rate of biomass washout ($DX$), where $D$ is the dilution rate (h⁻¹) and $X$ is biomass concentration (g/L). Therefore, $\mu = D$.

**Step 3: Write the steady-state mass balance for substrate.**

$$\dfrac{dS}{dt} = D(S_{in} - S) - \dfrac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} = 0$$

> *Logic:* Substrate enters at concentration $S_{in}$ and leaves at $S$, diluted at rate $D$. The rate of substrate consumption is $\mu X / Y_{X/S}$, where $Y_{X/S}$ is the biomass yield coefficient on substrate (g biomass / g substrate).

**Step 4: Solve for the steady-state substrate concentration $S$.**

Substitute $\mu = D$:

$$D(S_{in} - S) = \dfrac{D X}{Y_{X/S}}$$

$$\Rightarrow X = Y_{X/S}(S_{in} - S)$$

From Monod at steady state:

$$D = \mu_{max}\dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$$

Solve for $S$:

$$D(K_s + S) = \mu_{max} S$$

$$D K_s + D S = \mu_{max} S$$

$$D K_s = (\mu_{max} - D) S$$

$$\boxed{\,S = \dfrac{D K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}\,}$$

> *Logic:* As $D$ increases, $S$ increases (less time to consume substrate). At the limit $D \to \mu_{max}$, $S \to \infty$ — meaning the system cannot keep up, and **washout** occurs.

**Step 5: Define critical dilution rate.**

At the critical dilution rate, $S \to S_{in}$ and $X \to 0$:

$$D_{crit} = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}}$$

> *Logic:* Even at the maximum washout point, the rate is not exactly $\mu_{max}$ because of the saturation effect. The correction term $S_{in}/(K_s + S_{in})$ captures the fact that the Monod saturation is not yet 100 % at finite substrate concentrations.

**Step 6: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$D_{crit} = 0.5 \times \dfrac{20}{0.2 + 20} = 0.5 \times \dfrac{20}{20.2} = 0.5 \times 0.9901$$

$$\boxed{\,D_{crit} \approx 0.4950\ \text{h}^{-1}\,}$$

> *Logic:* With $S_{in} \gg K_s$ (a 100-fold ratio), $D_{crit} \approx \mu_{max}$, confirming the design rule that a chemostat should operate at $D \approx 0.3 \mu_{max}$ to avoid washout (≈ 60 % safety margin).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Scale-Up Power Number Criterion

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A 10 L lab bioreactor (impeller diameter $D_{imp,1} = 0.10$ m, impeller speed $N_1 = 300$ rpm) is to be scaled up to a 1,000 L production vessel maintaining **constant power per unit volume (P/V)**. Calculate the new impeller speed $N_2$ assuming **geometric similarity** with linear scale-up ratio $\alpha$.

---

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the volumetric scale-up ratio.**

$$\alpha = \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\dfrac{1000}{10}\right)^{1/3} = (100)^{1/3} \approx 4.6416$$

> *Logic:* Since volume scales as the cube of any linear dimension, the linear scale-up ratio is the cube root of the volumetric ratio.

**Step 2: Express the new impeller diameter.**

$$D_{imp,2} = \alpha \cdot D_{imp,1} = 4.6416 \times 0.10 = 0.4642\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Recall the power number correlation.**

For a Rushton turbine (6-blade) in a baffled tank, the **power number** $N_p$ (dimensionless) is approximately constant in the **turbulent regime** ($Re > 10^4$):

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_{imp}^5$$

> *Logic:* This is the classic power draw equation. $N_p$ depends on impeller geometry and Reynolds number but is roughly 5.0–6.0 for Rushton turbines in fully turbulent flow.

**Step 4: Apply the constant P/V criterion.**

$$\dfrac{P_1}{V_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{V_2}$$

$$\dfrac{N_p \rho N_1^3 D_{imp,1}^5}{V_1} = \dfrac{N_p \rho N_2^3 D_{imp,2}^5}{V_2}$$

Cancelling $N_p$ and $\rho$:

$$N_2^3 D_{imp,2}^5 \cdot V_1 = N_1^3 D_{imp,1}^5 \cdot V_2$$

**Step 5: Substitute scaling relationships.**

With $D_{imp,2} = \alpha D_{imp,1}$ and $V_2 = \alpha^3 V_1$:

$$N_2^3 (\alpha D_{imp,1})^5 V_1 = N_1^3 D_{imp,1}^5 (\alpha^3 V_1)$$

$$N_2^3 \alpha^5 D_{imp,1}^5 V_1 = N_1^3 \alpha^3 D_{imp,1}^5 V_1$$

Cancel $D_{imp,1}^5$ and $V_1$:

$$N_2^3 \alpha^5 = N_1^3 \alpha^3$$

$$N_2^3 = N_1^3 \cdot \alpha^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{\,N_2 = N_1 \cdot \alpha^{-2/3}\,}$$

> *Logic:* The impeller speed decreases upon scale-up (a counterintuitive but critical engineering rule). This is because the larger impeller sweeps a much larger volume per revolution — so to maintain P/V, the rotation rate must drop.

**Step 6: Compute the numerical value.**

$$N_2 = 300 \times (4.6416)^{-2/3} = 300 \times 0.3100 \approx 93\ \text{rpm}$$

> *Logic:* The new impeller speed at 1,000 L is only **93 rpm**, compared to 300 rpm at 10 L — a 3.2× reduction. This is essential to avoid excessive shear that would damage cells.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass Balance for SCP from Methane (Methylococcus capsulatus)

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

*Methylococcus capsulatus* grows on methane (CH₄) as the sole carbon and energy source, with the empirical biomass formula $\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2}$. Determine the **biomass yield per mole of CH₄** consumed, assuming ammonia (NH₃) as nitrogen source and O₂ as the terminal electron acceptor.

**Given:**
- $\mu_{max} = 0.30\ \text{h}^{-1}$
- $Y_{X/CH_4} = 0.85\ \text{g DM / g CH}_4$ (typical)
- Molecular weight of CH₄ = 16 g/mol
- Molecular weight of biomass ($\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2}$) ≈ 24.6 g/mol

---

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the stoichiometric reaction.**

$$a\,CH_4 + b\,O_2 + c\,NH_3 \rightarrow d\,\text{CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2} + e\,H_2O + f\,CO_2$$

**Step 2: Element balances.**

- **C balance**: $a = d + f$ ⟹ (1)
- **H balance**: $4a + 3c = 1.8d + 2e$ ⟹ (2)
- **O balance**: $2b = 0.5d + e + 2f$ ⟹ (3)
- **N balance**: $c = 0.2d$ ⟹ (4)

**Step 3: Normalize to $d = 1$ mol biomass.**

From (4): $c = 0.2$ mol NH₃.

Let $b$ and $f$ be expressed in terms of $a$:

From (1): $f = a - 1$.

Substitute into (3):
$$2b = 0.5(1) + e + 2(a - 1)$$
$$2b = 0.5 + e + 2a - 2 = 2a - 1.5 + e$$
$$b = a - 0.75 + \dfrac{e}{2}$$

Substitute into (2):
$$4a + 3(0.2) = 1.8(1) + 2e$$
$$4a + 0.6 = 1.8 + 2e$$
$$4a = 1.2 + 2e$$
$$e = 2a - 0.6$$

Substitute $e$ back into $b$:

$$b = a - 0.75 + \dfrac{2a - 0.6}{2} = a - 0.75 + a - 0.3 = 2a - 1.05$$

So the balanced equation is:

$$a\,CH_4 + (2a - 1.05)\,O_2 + 0.2\,NH_3 \rightarrow 1\,CH_{1.8}O_{0.5}N_{0.2} + (2a - 0.6)\,H_2O + (a - 1)\,CO_2$$

**Step 4: Apply the experimental yield constraint.**

The experimental yield is $0.85\ \text{g DM / g CH}_4$. Per mol CH₄ (16 g):

$$\text{Actual biomass} = 0.85 \times 16 = 13.6\ \text{g DM}$$

In moles: $13.6 / 24.6 = 0.553\ \text{mol biomass per mol CH}_4$.

So the **effective stoichiometric coefficient** of biomass per CH₄ is $1/a = 0.553$, giving $a = 1.808$.

**Step 5: Compute oxygen and water requirements.**

$$b = 2(1.808) - 1.05 = 2.566\ \text{mol O}_2$$
$$e = 2(1.808) - 0.6 = 3.016\ \text{mol H}_2O$$
$$f = 1.808 - 1 = 0.808\ \text{mol CO}_2$$

**Step 6: Final balanced equation.**

$$\boxed{\,1.808\,CH_4 + 2.566\,O_2 + 0.2\,NH_3 \rightarrow 1.0\,CH_{1.8}O_{0.5}N_{0.2} + 3.016\,H_2O + 0.808\,CO_2\,}$$

> *Logic:* The Respiratory Quotient $RQ = 0.808/2.566 \approx 0.315$ — a value characteristic of methanotrophs, reflecting the more reduced nature of CH₄ compared to sugars.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Fermentation Strategy for a Novel Product

| Decision Criterion | Precision Fermentation (PF) | Solid-State Fermentation (SSF) | Submerged Fermentation (SmF) | Gas Fermentation |
|--------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| **Product type** | Recombinant proteins, fine chemicals | Enzymes, flavors, bioactive | Cells, metabolites, SCP | Fuels, chemicals from CO₂ |
| **Capital cost** | High | Low–Medium | High | Very High |
| **Water usage** | High | Low | High | Medium |
| **Scalability** | Easy (mature) | Difficult (heat/mass transfer) | Easy | Medium |
| **Contamination risk** | Medium | Low (low $a_w$) | High | Low (aseptic) |
| **Yield (g/g)** | 0.05–0.30 | 0.10–0.50 | 0.20–0.50 | 0.10–0.40 |
| **Titer (g/L)** | 1–100 | n/a (per kg substrate) | 10–200 | 20–85 |
| **Substrate cost** | Glucose/glycerol | Agri-residues | Glucose/molasses | CO, CO₂, H₂ (free/waste) |
| **Best suited for** | Dairy/egg proteins | Traditional Asian foods, enzymes | Beer, wine, SCP | Climate-positive fuels |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]

**Define Precision Fermentation. List any two commercial products developed using this technology and the microorganism used in each case.**

**Model Answer (Structured):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Precision Fermentation (PF) is the use of genetically engineered microorganisms as **cell factories** to produce specific target molecules (proteins, enzymes, fats, flavors) with high purity, using the tools of **synthetic biology, recombinant DNA technology, and bioprocess engineering**.

**Two commercial examples (1.5 marks):**

1. **Whey protein (β-lactoglobulin)** — Produced by **Perfect Day Inc.** using the methylotrophic yeast ***Pichia pastoris* (Komagataella phaffii)** as the chassis. The gene encoding bovine β-lactoglobulin is inserted into *Pichia* under the methanol-inducible *AOX1* promoter.
2. **Soy Leghemoglobin ("heme")** — Produced by **Impossible Foods** using ***Pichia pastoris***. The heme protein imparts the characteristic meaty flavor and red bleed to the plant-based Impossible Burger.

> **[Total: 3 marks]**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**What are postbiotics and paraprobiotics? Differentiate between them with two points.**

**Model Answer (Structured):**

**Postbiotics (1.5 marks):**
Postbiotics are **preparations of inanimate (non-viable) microorganisms and/or their components** that confer a health benefit on the host. They include:
- Short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs: acetate, propionate, butyrate)
- Cell-wall fragments (peptidoglycans, teichoic acids)
- Exopolysaccharides
- Bacteriocins
- Vitamins (B12, K2)

**Paraprobiotics (1.5 marks):**
Paraprobiotics are **intact, non-viable (heat-killed or inactivated) microbial cells** that, when administered in adequate amounts, confer a health benefit. Example: heat-killed *Lactobacillus plantarum* LRCC5193.

**Differentiating Points (built into both definitions):**
| Feature | Postbiotics | Paraprobiotics |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| **Composition** | Mixture of metabolites and cell components | Intact inactivated whole cells |
| **Viability** | Not whole cells; may be molecules or fragments | Whole cells, killed |
| **Stability** | High (no live organisms) | High (no live organisms) |

> **[Total: 3 marks]**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Precision Fermentation (PF)** with a suitable flow diagram. Discuss the **microbial chassis** selection criteria with two examples each of yeast, bacteria, and filamentous fungi used in PF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **Process Analytical Technology (PAT)** framework and the role of **AI/ML in modern fermentation process control**. Provide at least four examples of PAT tools and explain the Model Predictive Control (MPC) loop. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A Part (a)

**Definition of PF (1 mark):**
Precision Fermentation is the **engineered production of specific functional food molecules** (proteins, fats, flavors) by microorganisms whose genomes have been edited using recombinant DNA technology, synthetic biology, and systems metabolic engineering to function as **biological factories**.

**Flow Diagram (1.5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pa1["TARGET IDENTIFICATION"] --> pa2["GENE ISOLATION AND SYNTHESIS"]
    pa2 --> pa3["VECTOR CONSTRUCTION WITH PROMOTER"]
    pa3 --> pa4["TRANSFORMATION INTO CHASSIS"]
    pa4 --> pa5["SCREENING AND EXPRESSION OPTIMIZATION"]
    pa5 --> pa6["FED BATCH BIOREACTOR SCALE UP"]
    pa6 --> pa7["DSP: CHROMATOGRAPHY, LYO"]
    pa7 --> pa8["GRAS, FSSAI, EFSA APPROVAL"]
```

**Chassis Selection Criteria (1.5 marks):**

A chassis is selected based on:
1. **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** status by FDA / **QPS (Qualified Presumption of Safety)** by EFSA.
2. **Post-translational modification (PTM) capability** — glycosylation, disulfide bond formation.
3. **Growth kinetics** — high $\mu_{max}$, tolerance to high substrate and product concentrations.
4. **Secretory capacity** — ability to secrete product extracellularly (simplifies DSP).
5. **Genetic tool availability** — strong promoters, well-characterized transformation methods.

**Examples (3 marks):**

| Chassis Type | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|--------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Yeast** | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* — used for **egg-white proteins (ovalbumin)** by Clara Foods | *Pichia pastoris* — used for **whey β-lactoglobulin** by Perfect Day |
| **Bacteria** | *E. coli* BL21(DE3) — used for **chymosin (rennet)** by Pfizer (first PF product) | *Corynebacterium glutamicum* — used for **amino acids** (lysine, glutamate) by Ajinomoto |
| **Filamentous Fungi** | *Aspergillus oryzae* — used for **glucoamylase** by Novozymes | *Trichoderma reesei* — used for **casein proteins** and cellulase |

> **[Total for part (a): 7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A Part (b)

**PAT Framework Definition (1 mark):**
Process Analytical Technology (PAT), introduced by the **US FDA in 2004**, is a **system for designing, analyzing, and controlling manufacturing through timely measurements of critical quality and performance attributes** of raw and in-process materials — with the goal of ensuring final product quality.

**Four PAT Tools (2.5 marks):**

1. **Near-Infrared (NIR) Spectroscopy** — measures glucose, ethanol, moisture in real time without sampling. Operates at 700–2500 nm.
2. **Raman Spectroscopy** — provides multiplexed fingerprint spectra; particularly useful in **mammalian cell culture** for distinguishing glucose, lactate, and ammonia.
3. **2D Fluorescence Spectroscopy** — exploits intrinsic fluorophores (NADH at 460 nm; tryptophan at 350 nm) to track **metabolic state** in real time.
4. **In-line Mass Spectrometry** — measures exhaust gas composition (O₂, CO₂, VOCs) for **respiratory quotient (RQ)** and metabolic flux analysis.

**AI/ML in Fermentation (1.5 marks):**

Modern fermentation uses AI/ML for:
- **Soft sensors** — predict hard-to-measure variables (product titer, by-product concentration) using NIR/Raman spectra + process data via **LSTM, Random Forest, or Gaussian Process Regression**.
- **Anomaly detection** — identify contamination or process drift in real time.
- **Dynamic optimization** — reinforcement learning agents that maximize $\text{Productivity} = P_v = (C_{P,f} - C_{P,0}) / (t_f - t_0)$.

**Model Predictive Control (MPC) Loop (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sp["SETPOINT: TARGET TITER, GLUCOSE"]
    ctrl["MPC CONTROLLER"]
    plant["BIOREACTOR PLANT"]
    sens["SENSORS: PH, DO, OD, NIR"]
    est["STATE ESTIMATION: KALMAN FILTER OR ML"]

    sp --> ctrl
    ctrl --> plant
    plant --> sens
    sens --> est
    est --> ctrl
```

> *Logic:* The MPC reads the **setpoint** (target), predicts future states using a process model, optimizes a cost function over a **receding horizon**, and applies control actions (feed, agitation, aeration). The **state estimator** (e.g., Kalman filter) reconciles sensor noise and unmeasured disturbances.

> **[Total for part (b): 7 marks]**

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define **Single-Cell Protein (SCP)**. Compare the nutritional profile of SCP from *Spirulina*, *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, and *Fusarium venenatum* in a tabular form. Discuss the **limitations of SCP**, especially the **nucleic acid problem** and how it is mitigated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Microbiota–Gut–Brain Axis (MGBA)** with a well-labeled schematic. Discuss the role of **psychobiotics** in mental health and the production of key **neurotransmitters** by gut microbes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B Part (a)

**Definition of SCP (1 mark):**
Single-Cell Protein (SCP) refers to the **dried biomass of microorganisms** — bacteria, yeasts, filamentous fungi, or microalgae — grown on various substrates (sugars, hydrocarbons, methane, CO₂, agri-waste) and used as a **protein source for human food or animal feed**.

**Comparative Nutritional Profile (3 marks):**

| Parameter | *Spirulina platensis* | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | *Fusarium venenatum* (Quorn™) |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Crude Protein (% DW)** | 60–70 | 45–55 | 45–55 |
| **Lipid (% DW)** | 5–10 | 5–10 | 10–15 |
| **Lysine content** | Moderate | High | High |
| **Methionine content** | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Nucleic acid (% DW)** | 4–6 | 6–12 | 5–8 |
| **Fiber (% DW)** | 5–10 | 5–10 | 10–20 (chitin) |
| **Vitamin B12** | Trace (pseudo-vitamin) | High | Moderate |
| **Color/Texture** | Blue-green, earthy | Beige, neutral | Fibrous, meat-like |

**Limitations of SCP (2 marks):**
1. **Nucleic acid problem** — high RNA content (6–12 % DW) leads to elevated uric acid → risk of gout, kidney stones.
2. **Cell wall digestibility** — especially in *Saccharomyces* and *Fusarium*.
3. **Flavor and color issues** — fishy, earthy, beery off-flavors.
4. **Heavy metal bioaccumulation** — microalgae can concentrate Cd, Pb, Hg.
5. **Consumer acceptance** — "eating microbes" perception barrier.

**Mitigation of Nucleic Acid Problem (1 mark):**
- Treatment with **NaOH at pH 9–10, 60 °C for 20–30 min** — degrades 80–90 % of RNA.
- **Hot water extraction (80 °C, 10 min)** followed by centrifugation.
- Endogenous **ribonuclease** activation at 55–60 °C.
- Recommended intake limit: **< 2 g nucleic acid/day** (WHO/FAO/UNICEF guidelines).

> **[Total for part (a): 7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B Part (b)

**Definition of MGBA (1 mark):**
The **Microbiota–Gut–Brain Axis (MGBA)** is a **bidirectional communication system** that integrates the central nervous system (CNS), the enteric nervous system (ENS), the autonomic nervous system (ANS), the neuroendocrine system, and the gut microbiota through **neural, hormonal, immune, and metabolic signaling pathways**.

**Schematic (1.5 marks):** [Refer to Section 4, Diagram 3 — MGBA]

**Key Components (1.5 marks):**
- **Vagus nerve** — major neural highway (10⁸ afferent fibers) carrying signals from gut to brain.
- **SCFAs** — acetate, propionate, butyrate produced by fermentation of dietary fiber; cross blood–brain barrier; modulate microglia.
- **Neurotransmitters** — GABA, serotonin (5-HT, 90 % gut-derived), dopamine produced by gut microbes.
- **Immune mediators** — cytokines (IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α) modulating neuroinflammation.
- **HPA axis** — cortisol regulation under stress.

**Role of Psychobiotics (2 marks):**

Psychobiotics are **probiotics that, when ingested in adequate amounts, produce or stimulate the production of neurotransmitters** that benefit mental health (anxiety, depression, cognition).

| Psychobiotic | Key Metabolite | Function |
|--------------|----------------|----------|
| *Lactobacillus rhamnosus* JB-1 | GABA | Reduces anxiety, modulates vagal tone |
| *Bifidobacterium longum* NCC3001 | Serotonin precursor | Reduces depression scores |
| *Lactobacillus helveticus* R0052 + *B. longum* R0175 | GABA, BDNF | Reduces cortisol, anxiety |
| *Lactobacillus plantarum* 299v | Dopamine precursor | Cognitive function |
| *Streptococcus thermophilus* | GABA | Mood stabilization |

**Mechanism (1 mark):**
1. Microbes produce neurotransmitters **in situ** in the gut.
2. These signals activate **vagal afferents** → reach nucleus tractus solitarius → limbic system.
3. SCFAs cross the blood–brain barrier and influence **microglial maturation** and **neuroinflammation**.
4. Tryptophan metabolism is shifted towards **kynurenine** or **serotonin** pathways depending on microbial composition.

> **[Total for part (b): 7 marks]**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in This Module (and How to Avoid Them)**
>
> 1. **Confusing precision fermentation with traditional fermentation.** Traditional fermentation is uncontrolled and uses naturally occurring microbes; precision fermentation uses **engineered chassis** with a **defined target molecule**. Marks deducted: up to **2/7**.
> 2. **Omitting the chassis selection criteria** in PF answers. Examiners expect a comparison of at least **3 chassis** with GRAS status, PTM capability, and growth rate. Marks deducted: up to **1.5/7**.
> 3. **Forgetting the nucleic acid problem in SCP answers.** A 7-mark question on SCP without the RNA/gout mitigation loses **2 marks** outright.
> 4. **Writing the Monod equation but not the $D_{crit}$ derivation.** Numerical derivation questions require **all 6 steps** — missing the final washout expression ($D_{crit} = \mu_{max} S_{in} / (K_s + S_{in})$) loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **Treating postbiotics and paraprobiotics as synonyms.** They differ on **intact-cell vs. metabolites**. Marks deducted: **1/3** in Part A.
> 6. **Skipping the schematic in MGBA questions.** A well-labeled diagram is **mandatory** and carries **1.5 marks** of the total 7.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — High-Density Bullet Points**
>
> ✅ **Precision Fermentation** = Engineered microbes as cell factories. Chassis examples: *Pichia*, *E. coli*, *Aspergillus*, *Corynebacterium*. First PF product: **chymosin (1990)**.
>
> ✅ **SCP Sources** = *S. cerevisiae* (50 % protein), *Spirulina* (65 %), *Fusarium venenatum* (Quorn, 50 %). Nucleic acid limit: **2 g/day**.
>
> ✅ **Probiotic Hierarchy** = Classical Probiotics → **Next-Gen Probiotics** (Akkermansia) → **Psychobiotics** (GABA, 5-HT producers) → **Postbiotics** (SCFAs) → **Paraprobiotics** (heat-killed cells) → **Synbiotics** (pro + pre).
>
> ✅ **MGBA** = Bidirectional. Vagus nerve + SCFAs + cytokines + HPA axis. 90 % serotonin is gut-derived.
>
> ✅ **PAT Tools** = NIR, Raman, 2D Fluorescence, Mass Spectrometry, Capacitance. FDA 2004 framework.
>
> ✅ **AI/ML in Fermentation** = Soft sensors (LSTM, RF, PINN) + Model Predictive Control (MPC) + Digital Twin + DBTL cycles.
>
> ✅ **Sustainability KPIs** = CO₂ footprint (kg CO₂e/kg), water footprint (L/kg), land use (m²·year/kg). Fermentation uses **95–99 % less land** than animal agriculture.
>
> ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize**:
> - Monod: $\mu = \mu_{max} S / (K_s + S)$
> - Washout: $D_{crit} = \mu_{max} S_{in} / (K_s + S_{in})$
> - Productivity: $P_v = \Delta C_P / \Delta t$
> - Scale-up (P/V): $N_2 = N_1 \alpha^{-2/3}$
> - OTR: $OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$
> - Logistic: $X(t) = X_{max} / (1 + e^{-k(t-t_m)})$
>
> ✅ **Commercial Landmarks to Cite in Answers**:
> - Perfect Day → whey (Pichia)
> - Impossible Foods → heme (Pichia)
> - Clara Foods → egg white (Trichoderma)
> - Solar Foods → Solein (Hydrogenomonas)
> - LanzaTech → ethanol (Clostridium autoethanogenum)
> - Quorn → mycoprotein (Fusarium venenatum)
>
> ✅ **Regulatory Bodies** = FDA (USA), EFSA (EU), FSSAI (India), JECFA (FAO/WHO).
>
> ✅ **Circular Bioeconomy** = 5Rs: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover, Redesign. Anaerobic digestion of spent biomass → biogas + digestate fertilizer.
>
> ✅ **Gas Fermentation** = Acetogens (*Clostridium autoethanogenum*) convert CO + CO₂ + H₂ → ethanol, 2,3-BDO, acetate. RQ ≈ 0.3–0.4.
